body:
## PR Reviewer Guide 🔍 #### (Review updated until commit https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/commit/270fc8d0f9a0c1330a14ab6eef236f250ab44557)
⏱️ Estimated effort to review: 4 🔵🔵🔵🔵⚪
🧪 PR contains tests
🔒 No security concerns identified
⚡ Key issues to review

Code Smell
The `StrictFormatter` class does not handle the case where `args` is not empty and `kwargs` is empty. It only checks if `args` is not empty, but does not check if `kwargs` is empty. Possible Bug
The `Prompt` class does not validate if the `input_variables` are actually used in the `template`. It only checks if the `template` is valid with dummy inputs. Test Coverage
The tests for `StrictFormatter` do not cover all possible scenarios, such as testing with an empty `template` or with a `template` that has multiple placeholders.

author: sjwhitmore

body: After examining the structure of the chained prompts (e.g. self ask with search & ReAct & Natbot), I think a less opinionated approach to the structure of the examples (e.g. a list of examples as strings rather than a list of examples as dicts with specific key-value pairs) is more likely to generalize across all prompts. I think the value of a list of examples is 1) readability 2) dynamically omitting an example if my input to the prompt is too long. I don't think we need to be too opinionated about the key/value pairs in the examples as we're already trusting the user to structure their prompt templates correctly... we don't want to make it too onerous to fit every example into the key/value input_variables + output_suffix paradigm. I'll push an update which simply accepts examples as a list.
author: sjwhitmore

body:
Pushed an example with the ReAct template... not quite sure how best to work in the example sizing into the Chain class.
Option 1: Add a num_examples argument to the __call__() method which takes a splice of the list of examples and uses that to initialize prompt in the _run() method. This feels like it muddies up the interface a bit, and also requires knowledge of the examples themselves and how long they are.
Option 2: Add a dynamic_prompt_sizing=True argument to the config, which detects if a prompt edges towards the token limit (or receives an over token limit error from an LLM) and automatically cuts an example. This feels like it might be a bit too magical / hiding too much config from the user.
Option 3: Explicitly let the user choose to use the example splicing in any further classes they define, and don't integrate it into the classes here.
Option 4: ?? Am I missing anything here?
author: sjwhitmore

body:
yeah i like the change to go to whole examples. we can also do both! expose different constructor methods depending on the use case

related to your last point around incorporating dynamic examples, what do you think about just having a different prompt class? this prompt class could be like `DynamicPrompt`, and could take in init a list of examples, and then the dynamic removal of examples (if necc) comes into play when you call `.format`?
author: hwchase17

body:
potentially theres two separate ideas/prs here:

- make it easy to specify a prompt from examples (what currently is here)
- make a separate dynamic prompt class (in follow up pr)
author: hwchase17

body:
makes sense! I like the idea of adding a DynamicPrompt in a separate PR.
I forgot to add the 3rd reason for having an explicit list of examples (which originally motivated my comment about this over text haha) -- making it easier to generate test data more easily.

body: Goal here is to dynamically choose examples best suited to a particular query. We accomplish this by running the examples through the embedding class when initializing the prompt, and storing them in a vector store backend. When the prompt format method is called, the class selects the most semantically similar examples to use in the template.

body:
I've been thinking more about naming for things like PythonREPL or BashProcess (and soon, Crawler etc)

- `primitives` is possible but to me connotes something even more basic, like integers or strings or something
- `instruments` isn't bad, but has multiple meanings
- `tools` is probably the best but is a good name for the intermediate chains
- `utilities` is also pretty good, sometimes means helper function imo
- `utensils` to me sounds like a tool that is very low level, a small hand-held tool

I think utilities or utensils and kind of lean toward the latter. anyway just some thoughts!

> so im down to check this in as is because i think its great, but one thing id like to think about before checking this in - how general could we make this? eg what is the most general prompt/chain that i could have that would work with this?

In the most general case, I think this verification becomes an agent, not a chain? You probably want to have an agent list its assumptions and then verify them with its tools, e.g. go to Wikipedia to look up a list of mammals.

This actually looks a lot like a parallel version of self-ask?

@hwchase17
author: andychenmagrathea-06e0a82f-34fc-48ca

> 
> This actually looks a lot like a parallel version of self-ask?
> 
> @hwchase17

oh i like that idea. in that framing the checkers are the tools then right? so what part of this chain is that checker/tool? not the whole part, because it also contains the question. maybe the next two parts at the end?
author: hwchase17

body:
Right so I guess there's two options:

1) If we turn LLMCheckerChain into an agent, the verification of each individual assumption is routed to the appropriate tool by the agent. I can see this as potentially being more robust, at the cost of increased compute (especially if you allow the agent to call itself; self-ask doesn't have as much of a problem because there's no fanout)

2) On the other hand, I do see benefits of keeping things as-is and having a fast-running verification chain as just a tool — seems like a cheap step to throw in if an agent is unsure of an answer.

Maybe we do both? #1 used when compute / latency isn't as much an issue, #2 more widely used to make things a bit more correct?
author: andychenmagrathea-06e0a82f-34fc-48ca

> 2. On the other hand, I do see benefits of keeping things as-is and having a fast-running verification chain as just a tool — seems like a cheap step to throw in if an agent is unsure of an answer.
> 
> Maybe we do both? #1 used when compute / latency isn't as much an issue, #2 more widely used to make things a bit more correct?

what would (1) look like exactly? would we change the prompt of the agent, to tell it to answer the question and then use a tool to fact check? what would that tool look like? would htat tool basically be the prompts here?

is there a common prereq for both (1) and (2)? namely the tool/chain that does the verfication? and in (2) its just a simple sequence of original LLM -> verificaton chain, and in (1) its a tool the agent has access to?

body:
> see the comment, i dont love that we remove the single input choice for lllm_chain....
> 
> what if we just kept one class but do something like
> 
> ```
> def run(self, *args, **kwargs) -> str:
>       if len(args) == 1 #check if single variable and use that
>      check **kwargs and use that if multi
> ```

Yeah I proposed something like this in the notion doc I sent you. The exact signature you wrote won't work though, it would have to return `Dict[str, str]` since if using `**kwargs`, you could expect multiple outputs. This def works but I think the main problems with using `*args` and `**kwargs` here are that 1) they are used mutually exclusively -- you need some error checking code that validates the input at runtime, 3) for single input chains, you have to unpack the output 4) if using one class, you still have the problem of `apply` function for single input chains expected a `List[Dict[str, Any]]`, which broke the convention of keeping `input` and `output` keys abstracted away from the user.

I think the benefit of having two separate base interfaces is that it's harder to get things wrong and send funky input to the `run` function. It also removes the need for excess validation logic (no need for `SimpleSequentialChain` to validate that all chains are single in/out, just make all the chains `SingleVariableChains`)

If there is a big quality of life improvement from not having to specify the input variables in `run` for the Multi chain (like `LLMChain`) when there is only one input key, we can add something like this to `MultiVariableChain`, we can probably use the python `multipledispatch` library to overload `run` (either directly in `Chain` or keeping the two classes).

So many options! maybe we should discuss in person

body:
hmmm so i think i see what you are getting at, first, some clarifying questions/assumptions:

1) are the chains you always want to join together going to be LLM chains? given that you are call .prompt it seems yes

If so, i think it is totally valid to want a "chain of prompts" basically where you just append to the previous prompt. but i would do it in a constructor, rather than its own chain. what i mean by that is, i would:

1. add a `extend_prompt` method to the base prompt class, which takes in a template and input vairables and extends the previous prompt (this is because there are different types of prompt classes, not all have a `.template`
2. add a constructor method to sequential chain that takes in an initial prompt and then follow up strings and creates a chain, eg

```
def from_prompt_chain(cls, llm: LLM, initial_prompt: PromptTemplate, extensions: List[Tuple[str, List[str]]]):
      chains = [LLMChain(llm=llm, prompt=initial_prompt]
      current_prompt = initial_prompt
      for template, input_vars in extensions:
            current_prompt = current_prompt.extend_prompt(template, input_vars)
            chains.append(current_prompt)
      return cls(chains)
```

does that make sense? happy to help with this - a few other folks had brought up similar things so i do think it would be good to get in!

template = """Between >>> and <<< are the raw search result text from google.

Extracted:<answer or "not found">
>>> {requests_result} <<<
Extracted:"""

What is the total size of their population ?

Agree with you its imperfect. I don't like to recreate the prompt here. Maybe we should store prompt inside the LLMResult and use that as the base to access the prompt given. (B) Do you think there is an issue with this approach?

body:
For instance, I'm using it like so:

```python
class FinishReasonChain(LLMChain):
    prefer_finish_on: Optional[str] = None
    
    def apply(self, input_list: List[Dict[str, Any]]) -> List[Dict[str, str]]:
        """Utilize the LLM generate method for speed gains."""
        response = self.generate(input_list)
        outputs = []
        for generation in response.generations:
            # Get the text of the top generated string.
            response_str = generation[0].text
            
            # If `prefer_finish_on_stop` then try to pick that
            if self.prefer_finish_on:
                responses = sorted(
                    generation,
                    key=lambda g: g.finish_reason == self.prefer_finish_on,
                    reverse=True
                )
                
                response_str = responses[0].text
            
            if self.verbose:
                langchain.logger.log_llm_response(response_str)
            outputs.append({self.output_key: response_str})
        return outputs

```

```python

    prefer_finish_on: Optional[str] = None
    
    def apply(self, input_list: List[Dict[str, Any]]) -> List[Dict[str, str]]:
        """Utilize the LLM generate method for speed gains."""
        response = self.generate(input_list)
        outputs = []
        for generation in response.generations:
            # Get the text of the top generated string.
            response_str = generation[0].text
            
            # If `prefer_finish_on_stop` then try to pick that
            if self.prefer_finish_on:
                responses = sorted(
                    generation,
                    key=lambda g: g.finish_reason == self.prefer_finish_on,
                    reverse=True
                )
                
                response_str = responses[0].text
            
            if self.verbose:
                langchain.logger.log_llm_response(response_str)
            outputs.append({self.output_key: response_str})
        return outputs
```

body:
I don't really understand when this would happen. Aren't we forcing each document to have a text key when we build the index? I refer to these lines in `/langchain/vectorstores/pinecone.py`:

```
for j, line in enumerate(lines_batch):
      metadata[j][text_key] = line
to_upsert = zip(ids_batch, embeds, metadata)
```

I guess what would make more sense is to raise an error if a document does not have a text key in the metadata, what do you think?
author: fpingham

body:
> I don't really understand when this would happen. Aren't we forcing each document to have a text key when we build the index? I refer to these lines in `/langchain/vectorstores/pinecone.py`:
> 
> ```
> for j, line in enumerate(lines_batch):
>       metadata[j][text_key] = line
> to_upsert = zip(ids_batch, embeds, metadata)
> ```
> 
> I guess what would make more sense is to raise an error if a document does not have a text key in the metadata, what do you think?

The issue isn't when there isn't a text key in the metadata, it's when it's the wrong text key. If you submit two documents with two different text keys and then query pinecone with something that returns both documents this method will throw a KeyError because it isn't expecting a different text key. Since this method essentially just filters out results by text key, I don't think it makes sense to log a warning or an error as it is intended behavior (from what I understand).
author: bernie-g

body:
I understand, thanks for the clarification. I think the intended usage is that all documents have the same `text_key` which is the one used in the retrieval (there should be no `KeyError`).

However, with the current configuration a `KeyError` is possible given that the user can send a `text_key` in the constructor and another in the `from_texts` method. This enables potential mismatches between the two text keys.

I think a neat solution for this would be for `from_texts` to use `self._text_key` to build the metadata. This way there would be no possibility of mismatch. This would make sense only if the intended usage is the one I mentioned above, am I correct in that @hwchase17? Or do we want to let users use different text keys for building and for querying?

body:
Got it. I also agree that separating it more would be better. Although I have some doubts about how this could look like.

1 - Would the run method of the Calendar tools look something like this? Where the wrapper doesn't handle the call to google API and where LLMGoogleCalendarChain handles classification and run_events.
```python
    def run(self, query: str) -> str:     
        google_calendar_chain = LLMGoogleCalendarChain(
            ...,
            query=query,
        )
        return google_calendar_chain.run(query)
```
 
- If so, wouldn't the wrapper tool lose its purpose of being a wrapper as most of the google API logic will be in the chain itself?

or

The other option I see is that the wrapper is the one that should be calling the google API.
That way it uses the chain to get the output in the right format then it uses the wrapper again to make the call to google API. Which I think makes more sense. Although, the chain wouldn't have access to the google API.

or

Maybe there should be better separation between wrappers and tools? But not sure about this one.

Let me know your thoughts :)

> @bpleahey I wonder what's the status of this PR?
> 
> Its seems to me that this functionality is quite important and I wanted to see if someone is working on it before implementing it myself.
> 
> I also wonder if you're making use of the exception in the next retry so it will be addressed by the llm?

Hey Alon, just following up. It looks like Harrison (or another contributor) has created general retry functionality when using openai's API here: langchain/llms/openai.py. I just hit a rate limit on OPENAI so I won't be able to do much today, but I just gave you access to the branch. You'll find a scratch folder with a notebook I was working on to catch errors. It's possible I missed some of the nuances with how the retries work, as I'm a relatively inexperienced software engineer, but I tried to provide some helpful info you can start working with.

Here is the list of TODOS I had:
- Restructure code to mirror new implementation for error handling: basic errors are now being thrown in langchain/chains/base.py:134. For consistency's sake, implementing a similar retry structure, or even overwriting the method could be a better approach then looping as I was doing. Particularly, the stop/wait implementation there seems cool, and is something I couldn't have come up with, especially not 5 months ago.
- Generate a more exhaustive list of SQL errors to be caught. I was attempting to do this using the notebook I created, and once I'm back under my rate limit am happy to keep helping with. SQLAlchemy is the library used in the SQL chain, and I included a link to the documentation in the base file (https://docs.sqlalchemy.org/en/20/core/exceptions.html).
- Most importantly, catching SQL queries that produce logical errors not caught at runtime. I'm happy to help with this part as well, depending on how intense my internship gets, as it seems to be a cool challenge depending on how much time I've got with work this summer.
- Fix minor bug with get_verbosity: get_verbosity in the base of the general chain was producing an error on line 25, saying langchain.verbose was not defined. I did a hacky fix of this by manually setting the field, and later reverted. It still seemed to be working in latest push so this might not really be an issue.

Best of luck with everything and happy to provide support—I like to finish things I've started, even if late.

Brendan

author: hwchase17

body:
Sorry, I should've been clearer :P

You can use it like this to quickly get multiple inputs, one at a time:

```python
from langchain.chains.multiple_outputs import GetMultipleOutputsChain
from langchain.llms.openai import OpenAI


llm = OpenAI(temperature=0, model_name="text-davinci-002")
print(GetMultipleOutputsChain(
    llm=llm,
    prefix="Generate a fictional person for me. Make sure to put all attributes in quotes.\n\n",
    variables={
        "first_name": "First Name",
        "last_name": "Last Name",
        "dob": "Date of birth",
        "gender": "Gender",
        "address": "Address",
    }
)({}))
```

Output:

```
{'first_name': 'Lydia', 'last_name': 'Smith', 'dob': 'June 12, 1990', 'gender': 'Female', 'address': '123 Elm Street'}
```

Extra complexity results from allowing the user to specify their own per-variable prompting for different types of LLM output (e.g. code blocks). For example, to get multiple pieces of code all at once:

    from langchain.chains.multiple_outputs import GetMultipleOutputsChain, VariableConfig
    from langchain.llms.openai import OpenAI


    PREFIX = """
    This is a Python function that calculates the Fibonacci sequence in a slow, recursive way.

    ```python
    def fib(n):
        if n == 0 or n == 1:
            return n
        return fib(n - 1) + fib(n - 2)
    ```

    Now rewrite this function in C++, JS, and Rust, along with accompany tests for each. Make sure to surround each code block with ```. Begin:

    """


    async def do():
        llm = OpenAI(temperature=0, model_name="code-davinci-002")
        print(GetMultipleOutputsChain(
            llm=llm,
            prefix=PREFIX,
            variable_configs=[
                VariableConfig.for_code("cpp", "C++ Code", language="cpp"),
                VariableConfig.for_code("cpp_tests", "C++ Test Code", language="cpp"),
                VariableConfig.for_code("js", "JS Code", language="js"),
                VariableConfig.for_code("js_tests", "JS Test Code", language="js"),
            ]
        )({}))


Fragment of result (colored text is LLM output - sorry I mentioned "Rust" in the prefix but forgot to add it in the list of outputs to get from the LLM):

![example](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1863868/215395617-ee294b11-f321-4a0b-888f-213acfb1195f.png)

Having this chain lets you ask for all that without needing special parsing logic every time you want multiple outputs out of the LLM.

If you've gotten it working but don't want to use so many LLM calls, you can also write a custom output parser and parse the generated outputs all at once. Here is me refactoring ZeroShotAgent to use get_action_and_input as the basis for an output parser, so that I can compare between asking it for one input at a time, versus asking it for many (and also to compare between returning "Final Answer" as a decision rather than as a special dialogue option):

https://github.com/amosjyng/langchain/commit/a4670feb#diff-a97b7d5316f498629288bf0ce84cb650892bc9eeb696c16cf51fafcb5cde789a

Results were mixed in that case, but I had wanted to experiment to see how it goes.

This is the general idea. Please let me know if that helps clear things up!

body:
How is this compatible with the global `OpenAICallbackHandler`? e.g. let's say you have multiple of the contexts mentioned in https://langchain.readthedocs.io/en/latest/modules/llms/examples/token_usage_tracking.html 

```python
import time
import asyncio
from langchain.callbacks import get_openai_callback
from langchain.llms import OpenAI

async def async_generate(llm):
    with get_openai_callback() as cb:
        resp = await llm.agenerate(["Hello, how are you?"])
        print(resp.generations[0][0].text)
        # not consistent as async execution will share the global handler
        print(cb.total_tokens)

async def generate_concurrently():
    llm = OpenAI(temperature=0.9)
    tasks = [async_generate(llm) for _ in range(10)]
    await asyncio.gather(*tasks)
```

I am not sure if this implementation is complete without handling callbacks properly

author: slavakurilyak

body:
Here's my explanation for this pull request, which can be used to update the docs:

---

This code consists of a Jupyter notebook that shows how to generate images from a prompt synthesized using OpenAI Language Learning Model (LLM) and Dall-E API. It can be divided into two parts: running as a chain and running as a tool with an agent.

The first part describes how to generate images from a prompt by creating an LLMChain, setting up the prompt using PromptTemplate, and running DallEAPIWrapper with the prompt. It uses a langchain package to accomplish these tasks.

The second part describes how to run the tool with an agent, which takes advantage of langchain's pre-built functionality and allows the user to interactively execute different actions. In this case, the tool is the Dall-E Image Generator, which can be used to generate images from a text description. The user provides a description of an image to the agent, which then generates the image using Dall-E API and returns it to the user.

To use the code, you would need to set up the OpenAI API key, as well as the required packages, such as Pydantic and OpenCV. You can then run the code in a Jupyter notebook or any other environment that supports it.

body:
@hwchase17 I made some change based on your suggestion.

- use OutputParser
- merge (retry and correct) into LLMChain
- ...and I modify the new chain to be about the correction only (LLMCorrectChain).

Here is an example how that look like now

```python
import os
from langchain.chains import APIChain, LLMChain, LLMRequestsChain
from langchain.llms import OpenAI
from langchain.prompts import PromptTemplate
from langchain.prompts.base import BaseOutputParser

llm = OpenAI(openai_api_key=OPENAI_API_KEY)

class IntegerParser(BaseOutputParser):
    def parse(self, text: str) -> int:
        return int(text)

template = """What is the price of {product}?"""
prompt = PromptTemplate(
    template=template,
    input_variables=["product"],
    output_parser=IntOutputParser()
)

chain = LLMChain(
    llm=llm, prompt=prompt,
    output_key="response",
    retries=2,
    correct_on_error=True
)

chain({"product": "an iphone"}, parse=True)
```

---

What do you think? (better I go any further)
author: BenderV

body:
Following our discussion @hwchase17

- rename parameters (now parser_retries & parser_correct_on_error)
- add possibility to use function as output_parser
- make parse=True the default (when there is an output_parser...)

```python
import os
from langchain.chains import LLMChain
from langchain.llms import OpenAI
from langchain.prompts import PromptTemplate

llm = OpenAI(openai_api_key=OPENAI_API_KEY)

def parse_integer(output):
    return int(output)

template = """What is the price of {product}?"""
prompt = PromptTemplate(
    template=template,
    input_variables=["product"],
    output_parser=parse_integer  # or could be directly "int" here
)

chain = LLMChain(
    llm=llm, 
    prompt=prompt,
    output_key="response",
    parser_retries=2,
    parser_correct_on_error=True
)

chain({"product": "an iphone"}) # => 699
chain({"product": "an iphone"}, parse=False) # "The price of an iPhone depends on the model and the retailer. Generally, the starting price for an iPhone 11 is around $699
chain.predict(product="an iphone")  # "The price of an iPhone...
chain.predict_and_parse(product="an iphone") # 699
```

---

Note 1: I need to add async support.
Note 2: This doesn't support list call (chain.apply...). This is because it would complicate the issue; what should we do if only 2/3 of parsing fail ? should we retry for those two ?
Note 3: We also need to change all return type to any... ? I don't know what we can do so it doesn't add complexity in all the codes. Is there a way to link it to the type of the prompt output_parser ?

body:
Hi, 
To answer the first question regarding how much generic are the operations done in base `VectorStore`:
I went through the documentation of all subclasses of `VectorStore`. 
in this PR I have added `setup_index`, `similarity_search_by_id`, `similarity_search_by_vector` to the base class, so the new functionalies are `creating an index or collection that represents a set of vectors`, `querying based on document ID`, `querying based on the vector`. so If all the subclass vector stores are able to do that, then we can add it to base class.

And here's what I found out of each different datastores:


**Weaviate**
- [supports indexes](https://weaviate.io/developers/weaviate/configuration/schema-configuration#class-object), I believe the terminology is called `Class object`
- [supports querying based on ID](https://weaviate.io/developers/weaviate/api/graphql/get#vector-search-operators)
- [supports querying based on vector](https://weaviate.io/developers/weaviate/api/graphql/vector-search-parameters#built-in-parameters)

**Qdrant**
- [supports indexes](https://qdrant.github.io/qdrant/redoc/index.html), I believe the terminology is called `Collection`
- [supports querying based on ID](https://qdrant.github.io/qdrant/redoc/index.html#tag/points/operation/get_point)
- [supports querying based on vector](https://qdrant.github.io/qdrant/redoc/index.html#tag/points/operation/search_points)

**Pinecone**
- [supports indexes](https://docs.pinecone.io/docs/indexes)
- supports querying based on ID, Pinecone doesn't have ID foreach document, but it can be stored in metadata and generated from langchain if no doc_id is provided
- [supports querying based on vector](https://docs.pinecone.io/docs/query-data#using-metadata-filters-in-queries)

**Milvus**
- [supports indexes](https://milvus.io/docs/create_collection.md#Create-a-Collection), I believe the terminology is called `Collection`
- supports querying based on ID, again Milvus doesn't have ID foreach document, but it can be stored in metadata and generated from langchain if no doc_id is provided
- [supports querying based on vector](https://milvus.io/docs/search.md#Conduct-a-vector-search) using data field in query time

As for Faiss, I'm not sure, haven't checked it thoroughly, there is a concept of index but I don't think you can actually filter the data or search by metadata.
please correct me on above data, but it seems most of the Vector stores already support the implemented functionality.
My bad for the title though, it should have been something more generic, But I only implemented `ElasticVectorStore`, new methods are not implemented for other datastores
author: yasha-dev1

body:
@yasha-dev1 I like the idea of filtering within vector stores! I can help with Qdrant implementation. I'm wondering how we could build some more complex queries (even thinking about simple AND/OR statements). 

I'd say the `VectorStoreFilter` should be common for all the engines, so from the user perspective there is no need to choose a different class if they decide to switch to a different provider. I imagine the `VectorStoreFilter` works as some sort of tree-like structure to keep all the criteria, and then they got parsed into the engine-specific structure. Either by a particular `VectorStore` or even better by some sort of engine parser, but definitely not in the `VectorStoreFilter` directly.
author: kacperlukawski

body:
@kacperlukawski  Yes, `VectorStoreFilter` should be a general interface for all vector stores. The best solution which comes to my mind is to implement ES-like Interface. for now only `And` operator is supported, but we can work on making it more general with `OR` operator. sth like ES [bool query](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/reference/current/query-dsl-bool-query.html)
Collaborations are more than welcome, I can add you as collaborator

body:
This looks very useful. The current way Agent loop is indeed kind of limiting. For example, here's a use case I'm having trouble addressing:

```
You are an AI writing assistant. You can help make additions and updates to a wide variety of documents.
Edit the document below to complete the task.

Tools:
------
You have access to the following tools:
{tool_list}

To use a tool, please use the following format:

... ommitted ...

Begin!

Document: 
{input.document}
Selection: {input.selection}
Instruction: {input.instruction}
Thought:
```

So, I want an Agent that can edit arbitrary text, given three inputs: a `document`, a `selection` within the document, and an `instruction` about how to update the selection. 

I suspect this PR would make my use case easier to solve.

body:
The original method did not know the specific size of the tokens, so depending on how the text is divided, an error could occur at run time.
So I changed it to calculate based on token size.

However, since the tiktoken package is required, it is not used by default so as not to affect existing projects.
author: Hase-U

body:
Fixed an issue where the token size was too large and this kind of error sometimes occurred
```
This model's maximum context length is 8191 tokens, however you requested 10001 tokens (10001 in your prompt; 0 for the completion). Please reduce your prompt; or completion length.
```


Weighted averaging is always performed when the token size exceeds the specified upper limit.
This is to protect the specification that the indices of texts and metadatas Lists passed to langchain's VectorStore etc. correspond. This ensures that the return value from the embedding class corresponds to each embedding in the given texts.

body:
I was actually thinking to implement something of a ~"DBMemory" module where the memory would be stored in an external database (whether serverless or not is a different story) and read it from there at every message. In my case I need it for Redis.

If that is implemented to be general enough (like it was [recommended above](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/1058?utm_source=pocket_reader#discussion_r1120273018)), then later any database could be added as a backend, including serverless ones like DynamoDB .

This is all needed to be able to have an application which handles multiple chat sessions in parallel and also the application can be scaled out as the chat sessions' state is stored in an external database (like Redis or DynamoDB) independently from the application. 
See a [similar use-case described here](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/issues/1115).

@hwchase17, are you okey with me making a PR for such a new memory module - initially supporting Redis.

Then potentially this DynamoDB one could be just a subset of that.
author: zoltan-fedor

body:
@zoltan-fedor I added what was recommend above as you can see [here](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/6ff413873e8500de77253071c47639322f7a9bc4/langchain/chains/serverless/memory.py). I made a ServerlessMemory class that is then used with the DynamoDB buffer. You should therefore be able to create a Redis buffer (with read, write, and clear operations) and just plug it in as the ServerlessMemory buffer.

If it essentially allows any database, including SQL and No-SQL, then we can change the name to something like DatabaseMemory.

I don't think there is a need for a separate `ServerlessMemory`, just the current  `ConversationBufferMemory` and `ConversationStringBuffer` need to be extended to write into a database ABC class, which then can be subclassed for DynamoDB, Redis or any other database.

If you can make this PR to be more general and integrate it into the current `ConversationBufferMemory` and `ConversationStringBuffer` methods, abstract out the database class, then I think that could work.

body:
@3coins,
I mostly agree and started to implement a similar pattern.

The part where I differ is that the existing `ConversationBufferMemory` and `ConversationStringBufferMemory` should be extended so it supports both the current operation (storing the session in memory) and also a database (in your implementation called `MessageStore`) as a backend. This way we don't need to ask the users to implement their own BufferMemory for the database they want to use.

This way all new type of memory in the future - they should all automatically work with any database implementation that follows that ABC.

EDIT: I have read your proposal again and it seems that you might be addressing this by _"Users who want to use a persistent store with ConversationBufferMemory can just create a new store, and ChatMessageHistory with this new store."_

author: 3coins

body:
@KBB99 
You current changes doesn't have all the changes suggested in my previous comments. If you follow the design proposal, I pretty much have all the code changes required and is smaller in size than the ones from the [langchain-aws-template](https://github.com/3coins/langchain-aws-template) repo.
author: 3coins

body:
@3coins,
I have included the database connection abstraction, so it can be cached and not recreated for each session (as the `MessageStore` is session-specific, the database connection can be shared across session).
I know that most db libraries would handle that automatically, but I am not sure that all will, hence I thought it made sense separating it out not knowing that what future database clients will be added.

See my implementation at https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/compare/master...zoltan-fedor:langchain:feature-memory-buffer-db

If you really want, I can remove the db abstraction.


Example use:
```
from langchain.memory.message_stores import DBMessageStore
from langchain.memory.message_stores.message_db import RedisMessageDB
from langchain.memory.chat_memory import ChatMessageHistory
redis_message_db = RedisMessageDB(url='redis://:PASSWORD@localhost:6379/0')  # this can be reused between session
store = DBMessageStore(message_db=redis_message_db, session_id='my-session')  # this is session-specific
message_store = ChatMessageHistory(store=store)
memory = ConversationBufferMemory(memory_key="chat_history", chat_memory=message_store, return_messages=True)
```

I have tested it locally on Redis with multiple sessions running in parallel, it worked well.

body:
Basically, at the time of implementation (and this is likely still the case but I have not looked recently), the HuggingFace generation interface does not provide for streaming. However, it does provide for two callbacks that can be used to implement things like streaming: LogitsProcessor ([doc definition](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/v4.35.0/en/internal/generation_utils#transformers.LogitsProcessor) / [doc use](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/v4.35.0/en/main_classes/text_generation#transformers.GenerationMixin.generate.logits_processor) / [source definition](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/main/src/transformers/generation/logits_process.py) / [source use](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/cc3e4781854a52cf090ffde28d884a527dab6708/src/transformers/generation/utils.py#L2627)) and StoppingCriteria ([doc definition](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/v4.35.0/en/internal/generation_utils#transformers.StoppingCriteria) / [doc use](https://huggingface.co/docs/transformers/v4.35.0/en/main_classes/text_generation#transformers.GenerationMixin.generate.stopping_criteria) / [source definition](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/main/src/transformers/generation/stopping_criteria.py) / [source use](https://github.com/huggingface/transformers/blob/cc3e4781854a52cf090ffde28d884a527dab6708/src/transformers/generation/utils.py#L2675)). Either one or both of these can be passed to the generation function via the keyword parameters `logits_processor` and `stopping_criteria` to received callback on every token.

They differ in that `logits_processor` is passed the latest logprobs (and its intended function is to mutate them), whereas `stopping_criteria` is called with the latest token after selection from the logprobs (and its intended function is to determine if generation is complete).

It looks like in my PR here, what I did was make a [very simple member function that redecoded the logits](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/pull/1222/files#diff-c79ffe136348560c76049fda7ba9d087df2ed0dce569336d3e427020c4056093R220) and handed the string off as it grew to a [more complex member function that performed streaming and stopping](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/pull/1222/files#diff-c79ffe136348560c76049fda7ba9d087df2ed0dce569336d3e427020c4056093R226). Rather than implementing a `stopping_criteria`, I mutated the logits to cause termination of the stream. I may have been unaware of stopping criteria, or I may have found it simpler in some way for me to do this. I then [wrap the function in a list and pass it as `logits_processor` when the generation function is called](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/pull/1222/files#diff-c79ffe136348560c76049fda7ba9d087df2ed0dce569336d3e427020c4056093R201) (here a pipeline object, which hands its parameters off).
author: xloem

body:
quick update: I implemented sending the query to the container through docker API's socket. This allows to truly send arbitrary commands and  fully simulates sending input through a TTY. The output is also read through the socket and separate in to stdin and stderr. 

This was necessary as sending commands using the basic `run` and `exec_run` docker does not allow to send complex multiline input and only allows for simple one line scripts. In the example notebook small bash script over multiple lines is sent to a spawned container. 

An other option would've been to use subprocess and call docker exec with pipe redirection. If the socket solution is too hacky I will fallback to this option.

body:
> Love to see this. Thanks for doing this as this was definitely on @tylerhutcherson and I's list. I have a couple high level comments.
> 
> 1. I think the name `Redis` would be better since people might assume `RediSearch` is a different thing. First part of the connection could use the `INFO MODULES` command to ensure that RediSearch is loaded. Another point is that we allow for vectors to be indexed in RedisJSON which would be confusing if people used the RediSearch container to use both modules without knowing they had to use the Redis-Stack container for this.
> 2. In relation, I think it would be nice to also have the ability to store as hash or JSON. This, I can do in a follow up PR though.
> 3. I left some performance pieces in there, but I'm happy to also do these as a follow up.
> 4. Lots of points on connection arguments. It would be ideal to match the semantics of the REdis-py library so that this is a similar drop in for our community.
> 5. I didn't see the addition of a optional package like `pip install langchain[redis]` which I think would be really nice. pinning a version to ensure these features are present is a must though.
> 
> Let me know what you think, we are happy to contribute some of these changes if you get stumped. Please reach out for clarification.
> 
> Awesome!

Thank you for the several comments you made.I have made fixes for the majority of the comments. 

Regarding the first point, I also initially thought the same way as you, not to use 'rediSearch'. However, considering that if the user directly uses a normal version of Redis and then uses this vectorstore, an error will occur, that's why it was changed to 'rediSearch'. The use of 'INFO MODULES' for checking, as you mentioned, is indeed a good point.

So do you think the following approach is more appropriate? If so, I will change it to 'Redis' as the file name and class name, looking forward to your re-review, thank you very much.
```python
if "search" not in [m["name"] for m in client.info().get("modules")]:
    raise ValueError("Could not use redis directly, you need to add search module"
                     "Please refer [RediSearch](https://redis.io/docs/stack/search/quick_start/)"
                     )
```
author: xinqiu

@xinqiu Thanks for this! I just pulled and tested all of the code. Beyond what @Spartee shared for future improvements and the comment I made above, we think adding another integration test would be helpful here:

```python
def test_redisearch_new_vector() -> None:
    """Test adding a new document"""
    texts = ["foo", "bar", "baz"]
    docsearch = RediSearch.from_texts(
        texts, FakeEmbeddings(), redisearch_url="redis://localhost:6379"
    )
    docsearch.add_texts(["foo"])
    output = docsearch.similarity_search("foo", k=2)
    assert output == [Document(page_content="foo"), Document(page_content="foo")]
```

Your suggestion about using the `client.info().get("modules")` cmd above is also good!

author: tylerhutcherson

body:
@tylerhutcherson @Spartee Another issue with redis-py has been discovered. When trying to query based on index_name in client.ft(self.index_name).search(redis_query, params_dict), there is no restriction and it appears that Redis is currently returning full results of a match.

Same problem like [this](https://github.com/RediSearch/redisearch-py/issues/117)
author: xinqiu

body:
> @tylerhutcherson @Spartee Another issue with redis-py has been discovered. When trying to query based on index_name in client.ft(self.index_name).search(redis_query, params_dict), there is no restriction and it appears that Redis is currently returning full results of a match.
> 
> Same problem like [this](https://github.com/RediSearch/redisearch-py/issues/117)

Interesting. This is not the `redis` Python library though, it's `redisearch` which is older. All search functionality has been ported to the official Redis library [here](https://github.com/redis/redis-py). Possible that has something to do with it.

Also - worth noting that Redis associates keys to an index using the `prefix` argument. So any Redis HASH that is prefixed by `prefix:` will be included in the index. It's more flexible that way. So if you have docs that need to be in different indices, you use a different prefix for those keys.

author: ash0ts

body:
For brevity this an example of usage

```
wandb_callback = WandbCallbackHandler(
    job_type="inference",
    project="langchain_callback_demo2",
    group=f"minimal_{session_group}",
    name="llm",
    tags=["test"],
)
manager = CallbackManager([StdOutCallbackHandler(), wandb_callback])
llm = OpenAI(temperature=0, callback_manager=manager, verbose=True)

# SCENARIO 1 - LLM
llm_result = llm.generate(["Tell me a joke", "Tell me a poem"] * 3)
wandb_callback.flush_tracker(llm, name="simple_sequential")
```

where `llm` can be replaced by a `chain` or `agent`

Wouldn't it make sense to make it a little more futureprof?

System/user/assistant separation is fine for current generation of chatgpt only. Wouldn't it make sense to split it based on function?
Like: instruction/eample_user/example_ai/user/ai?

What do you think?
author: Szpadel

body:
@Szpadel -- thanks so much for your comment! I really like the suggestion of having example_ai, example_user messages in addition to the ones we already have.

In terms of naming convention, we think System, Human, and AI wording is general enough to translate beyond OpenAI.

When you say `This way I think it could be then even mapped into single string for non chat models allowing common interface`, are you suggesting to have a single `Prompt` class that can parse the primitives you mentioned into either messages or string? We thought about that but thought it might impose too much structure.

In this RFC, we are kinda doing this with FewShotPromptTemplate, except the primitives are prefix, suffix, and examples. These get formatted as messages or as string depending on the impl `FewShotStringPrompTemplate` vs `FewShotChatPromptTemplate`

author: ever82

body:
@Prayforhanluo  If you use FastAPI server, refer to code as below
```python
@asynccontextmanager
async def lifespan(app: FastAPI):
    load_dotenv() # load .env variables, e.g. OPENAI_API_KEY / SERPER_API_KEY
    openai.proxy = os.getenv("HTTP_PROXY")
    api_logger.info(f"PROXY is setup at {openai.proxy}")
    yield
api_jumper = FastAPI(lifespan=lifespan)
```

To define your proxy in your .env file, e.g. "HTTP_PROXY="http://127.0.0.1:9991"

author: efriis

body:
Hi @pengqu123 thanks for your PR. We currently support custom callback handlers. To kick off events from intermediate results you can implement a `BaseCallbackHandler` and add it to the `CallbackManager`. These events can be sent to client through sockets, server side events or other mechanism.
See here for the code: https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/langchain/callbacks/base.py
See here for example implementation: https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/langchain/callbacks/streamlit.py

Do you explicitly need a generator interface or can you work with Callbacks? Would rather not add a new method to agent if possible.
author: agola11

body:
@agola11 Thank you very much for your discussion. In fact, we had used the callback approach before, but we encountered many pain points that needed to be investigated or resolved.
(1) SharedCallbackManager is a singleton and is not friendly to concurrency.
(2) When doing chat-related applications, we usually need to record the chatroom_id and message_id, and we need to find a way to pass them to the callback, and we need to support parallel scenarios.
(3) We need to confirm send_events in the custom callback one by one refer to stdout callback.

To be honest, at first, I also didn't think the generator was an elegant solution, but it pragmatically solved all the pain points.
(1) It directly returns results, although the results are yielded step by step, but the generator is a pythonic solution.
(2) Completely use LangChain as an independent module in the backend service.
(3) From a teamwork perspective, researchers only need to provide a better agent and an example, and engineers can directly wrap them into backend services. Researchers do not need to know about business scenarios such as chatroom_id and message_id, and backend engineers do not need to know about LangChain's code such as callbacks.
author: pengqu123

body:
@pengqu123 thanks for all of that context! Very helpful. Here are some of my points related to your discussion.

(1) Can you elaborate on this? SharedCallbackManager is actually a thread safe singleton but the problem is that custom callback handlers themselves might not be. There are a couple scenarios in which you might not want to use `SharedCallbackManager` in favor of the local version `CallbackManager`. One scenario is when you're using asyncio, which uses an event loop on one thread. To avoid collisions in callback handling, you can create a new agent with a new local callback manager on every new request. This callbackhandler can be instantiated with the detail of a particular request (i.e. client id, socket connection, etc.)
Here is a snippet in which we are using local callback managers in chatlangchain: https://github.com/hwchase17/chat-langchain/blob/master/query_data.py. ... The other scenario is when you don't want to worry about making your callback handlers thread-safe. In the context of a flask application or similar framework, each request is usually handled by its own worker thread, to not worry about thread safety you can avoid global state by instantiating new agents, callback managers, etc for every request.

(2) Can you expand more on parallel scenarios?

(3) Not sure I follow, sorry!

Hope we can figure something out here and sorry if I'm not fully understanding your use case. We think callback handling is generally a more flexible solution for most scenarios and would like to use it as much as possible to avoid having multiple methods doing similar things. If that's totally not possible for your use case then we can think about adding generators in some form.
author: agola11

body:
@agola11 Thanks for your reply! Let me try to explain（2）and（3）

(2) Can you expand more on parallel scenarios?
There are many requests sent to the backend, and to simplify, each request include query, chatroom_id, and message_id. Therefore, if we send intermediate results out in the custom callback, we must include the chatroom_id and message_id in the response. This way, the generated text of the response can appear in the correct chatroom.

(3) Not sure I follow, sorry!
“We need to confirm send_events in the custom callback one by one refer to stdout callback.” 

Sorry, let me explain this in more detail. This actually describes the work status of backend engineers in the teamwork I mentioned earlier. Because when the backend engineer customizes the callback, they will carefully write the code for sending messages according to langchain/callbacks/stdout.py. However, in fact, this work efficiency is not high. We can imagine that a researcher implements an example and displays the result through the console output. The backend engineer then has to learn how to customize the callback carefully, but in fact, now we can collect intermediate results through return_intermediate_steps=True. Why not yield them through a generator? because while callback handling is a very flexible solution, in real-world work scenarios, both researchers and backend engineers don't want to study it unless it's absolutely necessary.
author: pengqu123

body:
FYI I had exactly the same need and came up with a quick-and-dirty solution: overriding `__call__` and `_call` in the `AgentExecutor` to yield the intermediate results.
See this gist: https://gist.github.com/didmar/112b5886993cea00a6d88c3df39771b6
Would be great to have something like that cleanly integrated.

body: I was thinking about this seeing you Twitter announcement today. Right now the existing memory classes have in internal representation of the memory, but the chat-style interfaces pulls the memory/chat into the public API. We ran into in working on the AWS deployment template for LangChain where we needed to build Memory subclasses that stored the chat in DynamoDB. The chat-style interface makes models with memory stateless, which simplifies a lot. At the same time, there can be memory implementations where it is not possible to make the memory part of the public API.

body:
Interesting... so I'm guessing this error occurred because you had the `schema` parameter set in `SQLDatabase`, right? I never ran into this error because I set the schema in the connection URI, like `mariadb+pymysql://user:pass@host/schema`. Personally, my preference would be to let users specify a schema in the connection string rather than handling it in LangChain. 

I'm not too familiar with all the dialects, but it appears that `SET search_path TO` is specific to postgres. In MySQL/MariaDB it's `USE schema`, in Oracle it's `ALTER SESSION SET CURRENT_SCHEMA=schema`. Specifying the database/schema using sqlalchemy's `connect_args` when creating the engine also varies by dialect. 
I think it's better to let the user handle this, otherwise we'd have to go through and detail all the specific variations.

So I'm in favor of removing [lines 178-179](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/langchain/sql_database.py#L178-L179), or maybe even just adding a check for postgres there. I guess not many people specify schema in `SQLDatabase` anyway, otherwise we'd have seen a lot more of these issues. 
What do you think @hwchase17 @fpingham?

Added changes to this, main thing from a user perspective is that you don't add the embedding method at vector store init, but the embedding object. [Here's an example notebook](https://gist.github.com/jamescalam/6435c4925a3cbf9328e8b566e0969f2d) showing how and where to use this.

Here's the breaking change mentioned above:

```python
# NEW init code
vectorstore = Pinecone(
    index, embeddings, text_field
)

# OLD init code
vectorstore = Pinecone(
    index, embeddings.embed_query, text_field
)
```

body:
@alexprice12 I agree with you, it's basically a fast and ugly hack. In my project, I ended up replacing this agent with several custom chains, where each chain does its own little thing (but does it very well, each with few-shot examples):
1. Predicts a tool
2. If search, predicts queries
3. Map-reduce style (also custom) response construction

I would suggest replacing the original Langchain's one-step JSON with a two-step JSON-free approach (first generate just the tool name, then generate what the tool needs - in different chains).

body:
I modified the prompts:

```python
FORMAT_INSTRUCTIONS = """RESPONSE FORMAT INSTRUCTIONS
----------------------------

When responding please, please output a response in this format:

thought: Reason about what action to take next, and whether to use a tool.
action: The tool to use. Must be one of: {tool_names}
action_input: The input to the tool

For example:

thought: I need to send a message to xxx
action: Telegram
action_input: Send a message to xxx: I don't agree with that idea
"""
```

The agent responds in the following format:

```
thought: I can use Telegram to send a message to xxx with a poem.
action: Telegram
action_input: Send a message to xxx with the following poem:

Oh hey there, it's me
(truncated)
```

The modified `parse` method of the 'BaseOutputParser`:
```python
    def parse(self, text: str) -> Any:
        """
        More resilient version of parser.

        Searches for lines beginning with 'action:' and 'action_input:', and ignores preceding lines.
        """
        cleaned_output = text.strip()

        # Find line starting with 'action:'
        action_match = re.search("^action: (.*)$", cleaned_output, re.MULTILINE)
        if action_match is None:
            raise ValueError(f"Could not find valid action in {cleaned_output}")
        action = action_match.group(1)

        action_input_match = re.search(
            "^action_input: (.*)", text, flags=re.MULTILINE | re.DOTALL
        )
        if action_input_match is None:
            raise ValueError(f"Could not find valid action_input in {cleaned_output}")
        action_input = action_input_match.group(1)
        return {"action": action, "action_input": action_input}
```

body:
Yes good point. I was originally thinking about pushing this pull request to establish the parallel abstraction to start, and then adding the asynchronous feature in a follow-up pull request (where asynchronous execution can be toggled on or off when initializing the ParallelChain), but I can add the asynchronous feature in this one, if you think it would be more appropriate. 

The parallel abstraction itself has some open design choices I'd like to get your perspective on, reproduced here: 
1. Would it make more sense to have all the chains define their own input variables manually, or should we let the parallel chain define the input variables once and propagate that to all the child chains? An argument for the former would be that it follows the existing convention I've seen in defining chains so far. An argument for the latter would be that it would allow the user to define the input variables to the entire parallel block in one place (which would make the ParallelChain abstraction more powerful), without having to copy the same input variables for all child chains.
2. Any thoughts on whether it makes sense for this to be refactored in as a subcomponent of the MapReduceChain? The main reason for replacing the map component of the mapreduce by a ParallelChain would be that users can use ParallelChain for use cases other than applying the same llm for reducing a set of documents with the same prompt - they would instead be able to use multiple chains with different prompts each.
3. Any thoughts on how we would want to support multiple outputs for each child chain, i.e. how we would want to format the output of the ParallelChain? One possibility is to do a nested dict, like:
```
{
    'output_for_chain_1: {'chain_1_output_1': str, 'chain_1_output_2': str, ... },
    'output_for_chain_2: {'chain_2_output_1': str, 'chain_2_output_2': str, ... },
}
```
or flatten the keys with a delimiter, like:
```
{
    'output_for_chain_1/chain_1_output_1': str, 'output_for_chain_1/chain_1_output_2': str, ... },
    'output_for_chain_2/chain_2_output_1': str, 'output_for_chain_2/chain_2_output_2': str, ... },
}
```
author: mbchang
mbchang

body:
I would very strongly not want to use multithreading here. Makes it very easy to mess things up bc we have to worry about thread safety. Plus, we already offer an async api.

A few comments:
1) I don't like the term "parallel" here -- concurrency does not imply parallelism esp in python where only one thread can execute at a time due to GIL.
2) I'm not sure this is needed -- we can already run multiple chains concurrently with asyncio... see this agent example for instance https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/docs/modules/agents/agent_executors/examples/async_agent.ipynb

llmchain  example here: 
https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/docs/modules/chains/generic/async_chain.ipynb
author: agola11

body:
Hey @agola11, thanks for the comments, and thanks for introducing the async api to the LangChain library. I have rewritten the multithreading code to only use the same `asyncio` functions that have been used in the rest of the repo. I'll address some of your comments below. I also get a chance to chat with @hwchase17 on this at the F.Inc. office hours. Let me know what you guys think.

# Why `ParallelChain` is needed 
One goal of LangChain is to provide abstractions that makes it easy for users to compose LLMs together. The two main topologies for composition are composition in series and composition in parallel. These two topologies are the building blocks of building complex networks, including electronic circuits and neural networks. LangChain already has `SequentialChain` for composition in series, but we do not have an abstraction for composition in parallel, which is the problem that this pull request addresses.

There are many cases where the user might want to process the same input with multiple chains in parallel.
 - processing different chunks of a document 
 - asking different questions about the same observation (hypothesis generation)
 - grouping a set of independent chains together that operate on the same inputs

What we want is a way to abstract the independent execution of multiple related chains together - the parallel topology. This would allow us to treat such a group of chains as itself a chain that can be composed as a primitive with other chains. For example, in the docs I've given an example of how we can nest a `ParallelChain` inside another `ParallelChain`.

## The difficulties with the current async api
Ideally, the code the user writes should separate the chain network topology from how the network is executed. However, the current async api for LangChain does not yet do enough to support this. The current async API requires the user to add the async decorator all the way up to the top-level function, which prevents the user from creating nested chain networks easily. This is because it entangles the code for expressing the topology with the code for how the topology should be executed. The user may want to express a parallel topology to connect various LLMs without wanting to execute them asynchronously.

## `ParallelChain` as a solution
The goal of the `ParallelChain` is to be an abstraction of the parallel topology and is useful whether or not the child chains in the `ParallelChain` are executed concurrently. By providing an abstraction of the parallel topology, `ParallelChain` abstraction allows the user to toggle concurrency on or off for a group of chains connected in parallel, without changing the code the user needs to write.

## Updates
1. I have updated the code to remove multithreading, using only the same `asyncio` functions that have been used in the rest of the repo.
2. After talking with @hwchase17, I changed the output of the `ParallelChain` to be a nested dict:
```
{
    'output_for_chain_1: {'chain_1_output_1': str, 'chain_1_output_2': str, ... },
    'output_for_chain_2: {'chain_2_output_1': str, 'chain_2_output_2': str, ... },
}
```
3. I removed the restriction for all child chains to take the same inputs, which would allow for more flexible composition.

# Naming
I used the term "parallel" to refer to the topology, as in "parallel circuit". The naming is intended to refer to how the LLMs are *connected* not how they are *executed*. However, I agree with you that there may be a potential confusion that "parallel" may refer to "multiprocessing". Happy to hear your suggestions on this.

author: hwchase17

body:
> > This implementation still allows using the SQLAlchemy standard way to get all tables which exist in the database by using the original function get_all_tables().
> 
> maybe im missing someting, but doesnt this remove the get_all_tables function entirely?

You are right.  For some reason in my testing it looked like Python was honoring overloading of the base class function based on the number of parameters used in get_all_tables(), but evidently this is not the case.  I'm happy to refactor if there is a better pattern to follow here.  Basically, the root issue I am trying to solve is get_all_tables() does not honor the 'include_tables' nor 'ignore_tables' properties of the instantiated class object.  This causes extra overhead by processing tables which should be ignored (very painful in my case where I have lots of tables in the database), and possibly allows access to tables which should be off limits for whatever reason.

body: @hwchase17 Yup. Just to confirm, you don't want to leave any access to the base class function of the same name?  It returns all possible tables.  This similar local function returns all curated (non-filtered) tables (taking into account the include and ignore statements from when the object was created).

This similar local function returns all curated (non-filtered) tables (taking into account the include and ignore statements from when the object was created).

sorry i still dont think im understanding. if we reverted `get_usable_table_names` back to `get_table_names` would this change the functionality at all?
author: hwchase17

body:
@hwchase17 get_tables_names() exists in the SQLAlchemy base class, as well was the HEAD version of the wrapper class.  They differ in functionality.  The base class version returns all tables in the database.  The wrapper class returns a more constrained view of tables be removing tables which were specifically declared to be ignored (or explicitly included) at time of object creation.  

The bug is introduced in this line:
`self._all_tables = set(self._inspector.get_table_names(schema=schema))`
...in the wrapper class, which seems to assume it is using the function defined in the wrapper class but in actuality is using the function defined in the SQLAlchemy base class.  Since these functions are named the same, I can see why that confusion was made. So this is why I took further steps to distinguish the function names in my changes.

It seems logical that if we ask for a catalog of the known universe (of tables) that we would adhere to any specific exclusions indicated at the time the object was created. This logic also follows over to the situation I am trying to rectify, which is that currently the wrapper class is trying to collect metadata about tables which are irrelevant to the operations of the object (its trying to query information about tables which were excluded from the object's context).  In situations where there are a lot of tables, this wastes considerable setup time unnecessarily.

body:
Fair, you're right! 

The ConstitutionalChain assumes that it has access to the prompt of its input, because it passes it as a kwarg to run when it runs self.critique_chain.run and self.revision_chain.run:

```
            raw_critique = self.critique_chain.run(
                **input_prompt=input_prompt**,
                output_from_model=response,
                critique_request=constitutional_principle.critique_request,
            )
            critique = self._parse_critique(
                output_string=raw_critique,
            ).strip()

            # Do revision

            revision = self.revision_chain.run(
                input_prompt=input_prompt,
                output_from_model=response,
                critique_request=constitutional_principle.critique_request,
                critique=critique,
                revision_request=constitutional_principle.revision_request,
            ).strip()
            response = revision 
```
It uses more than just the response; it also uses the prompt input. 

If you give me some guidance for your preferred solution, I'd be happy to make a separate class which work on chains as a whole or adopt some other solution!

author: hwchase17

body:
@hwchase17 @chutcheson relatively new to LangChain syntax and design, but here is what I was thinking.
Option 1: Treat ConstitutionalChain as a data flow element, meaning it can be instantiated with other chains like Retrieval chains, LLChains etc. on the lines of @chutcheson thinking.
```
from langchain.chains.constitutional_ai.base import ConstitutionalChain
from langchain.chains import SequentialChain
from langchain.chains import LLMChain

qa_chain = RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(llm=OpenAI(), chain_type="stuff", retriever=docsearch.as_retriever())

principles = ConstitutionalChain.get_principles(["illegal"])
constitutional_chain = ConstitutionalChain.from_llm(
    chain= **qa_chain**
    constitutional_principles=principles,
    llm=OpenAI(temperature=0),
    verbose=True,
)
```
Option 2: Draw a distinction between Data flow and Control flow chains. Control flow chains decide the logical flow of the chains/pipelines e.g. SequentialChains, RouterChains etc. based on some decisions or logical operations.
This will help build more complex flow / pipelines, where output of one chain is the input to constitutional or moderation chains.
```
from langchain.chains.constitutional_ai.base import ConstitutionalChain
from langchain.chains import SequentialChain
from langchain.chains import LLMChain

qa_chain = RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(llm=OpenAI(), chain_type="stuff", retriever=docsearch.as_retriever())

principles = ConstitutionalChain.get_principles(["illegal"])
constitutional_chain = ConstitutionalChain.from_llm(
    chain= LLMChain(llm=OpenAI(temperature=0))
    constitutional_principles=principles,
    llm=OpenAI(temperature=0),
    verbose=True,
)
constitutional_chain.input_key = "output_text"
sq = **SequentialChain(chains=[qa_chain, constitutional_chain])**
```
What are your thoughts? In the meanwhile, are there any suggested workarounds for this scenario.

body:
I looked into the langchain code but unfortunately wasn't able to pinpoint the issue in a jiffy given the complexity of its inner workings. Instead, I ended up implementing a workaround that tracks token usage via [`tiktoken`](https://pypi.org/project/tiktoken/):

```
from typing import Any, Dict, List

from langchain.callbacks.base import AsyncCallbackHandler
from langchain.schema import LLMResult
from tiktoken import encoding_for_model


class AsyncUsageTrackingCallbackHandler(AsyncCallbackHandler):
    def __init__(self, model_name: str) -> None:
        self.model_name = model_name
        self.tiktoken = encoding_for_model(model_name)
        self.tokens_sent = 0

    def always_verbose(self) -> bool:
        return True

    # TODO: The following is all we would need IN THEORY, however it doesn't
    # work because the token usage is not tracked correctly, see
    # https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/1924#issuecomment-1577142911
    # def on_llm_end(self, response: LLMResult, **kwargs) -> None:
    #     track_tokens_from_llm_result(self.model_name, response)
    #
    # Instead, we manually track the tokens we send and receive using tiktoken
    async def on_llm_start(self, serialized: Dict[str, Any], prompts: List[str], *args, **kwargs) -> Any:
        self.tokens_sent += sum([len(self.tiktoken.encode(prompt)) for prompt in prompts])

    async def on_llm_end(self, response: LLMResult, **kwargs) -> None:
        tokens_received = sum(
            [len(self.tiktoken.encode(g.message.content)) for generations in response.generations for g in generations]
        )
        print(f'[{self.model_name}] tokens sent: {self.tokens_sent}, tokens received: {tokens_received}')
```

body:
This callback can be used by both OpenAI and ChatOpenAI models:
```python
from langchain.callbacks.streaming_stdout import StreamingStdOutCallbackHandler
from langchain_community.callbacks.openai_info import get_openai_token_cost_for_model
from langchain.schema import LLMResult
from tiktoken import encoding_for_model
from langchain_core.messages import BaseMessage

class TokenUsageTrackingCallbackHandler(StreamingStdOutCallbackHandler):
    def __init__(self, model_name: str) -> None:
        self.model_name = model_name
        self.tiktoken = encoding_for_model(model_name)
        self.tokens_sent = 0

    def always_verbose(self) -> bool:
        return True

    def on_llm_start(
            self, 
            serialized: Dict[str, Any], 
            prompts: List[str], 
            *args, 
            **kwargs
        ) -> Any:
        self.tokens_sent += sum([len(self.tiktoken.encode(prompt)) for prompt in prompts])

    def on_chat_model_start(
            self,
            serialized: Dict[str, Any],
            messages: List[List[BaseMessage]],
            **kwargs: Any,
        ) -> None:
        self.tokens_sent += sum([len(self.tiktoken.encode(prompt[0].content)) for prompt in messages])

    def on_llm_end(self, response: LLMResult, **kwargs) -> None:
        tokens_received = sum(
            [len(self.tiktoken.encode(g.text)) for generations in response.generations for g in generations]
        )
        input_token_cost = get_openai_token_cost_for_model(model_name=self.model_name, num_tokens=self.tokens_sent)
        output_token_cost = get_openai_token_cost_for_model(model_name=self.model_name, num_tokens=tokens_received, is_completion=True)
        total_cost = input_token_cost + output_token_cost
        print(f'\n[{self.model_name}]\n\tTokens sent: {self.tokens_sent}\n\tTokens received: {tokens_received}\nTotal Cost (USD): ${total_cost}')
```

Above callback handler can be called as below:
```python
ChatOpenAI(model_name='gpt-3.5-turbo-1106', temperature=0.3, streaming=True, callbacks=[TokenUsageTrackingCallbackHandler(model_name='gpt-3.5-turbo-1106')])
```

@meet1919 

`self.tokens_sent += sum([len(self.tiktoken.encode(prompt[0].content)) for prompt in messages])
`
in your code only count the first item in messages, to get all tokens from system messages and human messages : 

```
    def on_chat_model_start(
            self,
            serialized: Dict[str, Any],
            messages: List[List[BaseMessage]],
            **kwargs: Any,
        ) -> None:
        token_count_list = []
        [[token_count_list.append(len(self.tiktoken.encode(m.content))) for m in prompt] for prompt in messages]
        self.tokens_sent += sum(token_count_list)
```

In ```BaseChatOpenAI._astream``` method, usage dict was only processed only when ```if len(chunk["choices"]) == 0```, while some of Chat Providers (like Zhipu AI) doesn't follow this protocol, so you might need to modify conditions here like:
```if len(chunk["choices"]) == 0 or chunk["choices"][0].get("finish_reason") is not None:``` to make sure ```_atream``` get usage dict properly.

body:
Just noticed, it applies to this but also the current LLM cache implementation:
The hash() function in python is (by default) not repeatable between runs. 
see:
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/27522626/hash-function-in-python-3-3-returns-different-results-between-sessions

This only seems to be used in for the redis database, but I noticed your implementation is using the same hash logic...

I am not sure what the best replacement is.

> * Ensure we inform users of this change as much as possible, as it is a large breaking interface change for most providers. (changelog, blog post, Twitter?)

thanks for doing this! is a very big pr, really appreciate it :)

my gut reactions: would love to avoid breaking changes, would love to keep interface as similar to underlying apis as possible (makes easier for people to use), would love to see the auth/env logic used automagically

i get the desire for a standard for an interface. but i also thinking `keep interface as similar to underlying apis as possible (makes easier for people to use)` is important. how about this for a thought (and its late so could be stupid): for things like `model_id`, lets make that a property that each class that to implement rather than an input. that way, the input can still be whatever the underlying api is (eg model_name) but we can access the model_id in a standard way. or, if the goal is to enable people to initialize the class in a standard way, lets add some pydantic magic to alias  model_name to `model_id` and the like. i want to make burden of switching from using udnerlying apis to langchain as small as possible, and also minimize breaking changes

author: Mlr75

body:
# NotionDBLoader

`NotionDBLoader` is a Python class for loading content from a Notion database. It retrieves pages from the database, reads their content, and returns a list of `Document` objects.

## Requirements

- A Notion Database
- Notion Integration Token

## Setup

### 1. Create a Notion Table Database

Create a new table database in Notion. You can add any column to the database and they will be treated as metadata. For example you can add the following columns:

- Title: set Title as the default property.
- Categories: A Multi-select property to store categories associated with the page.
- Keywords: A Multi-select property to store keywords associated with the page.

Add your content to the body of each page in the database. The NotionDBLoader will extract the content and metadata from these pages.

### 2. Create a Notion Integration

To create a Notion Integration, follow these steps:

1. Visit the (Notion Developers)[https://www.notion.com/my-integrations] page and log in with your Notion account.
2. Click on the "+ New integration" button.
3. Give your integration a name and choose the workspace where your database is located.
4. elect the require capabilities, this extension only need the `Read content` capability
5. Click the "Submit" button to create the integration.

Once the integration is created, you'll be provided with an `Integration Token` (API key). Copy this token and keep it safe, as you'll need it to use the NotionDBLoader.

### 3. Connect the Integration to the Database

To connect your integration to the database, follow these steps:

1. Open your database in Notion.
2. Click on the three-dot menu icon in the top right corner of the database view.
3. Click on the "+ New integration" button.
4. Find your integration, you may need to start typing its name in the search box.
5. Click on the "Connect" button to connect the integration to the database.

### 4. Get the Database ID

To get the database ID, follow these steps:

1. Open your database in Notion.
2. Click on the three-dot menu icon in the top right corner of the database view.
3. Select "Copy link" from the menu to copy the database URL to your clipboard.
4. The database ID is the long string of alphanumeric characters found in the URL. It typically looks like this: https://www.notion.so/username/8935f9d140a04f95a872520c4f123456?v=.... In this example, the database ID is 8935f9d140a04f95a872520c4f123456.

With the database properly set up and the integration token and database ID in hand, you can now use the NotionDBLoader code to load content and metadata from your Notion database.

## Usage

NotionDBLoader is part of the `langchain` package's document loaders. You can use it as follows:

```python
from langchain.docstore.document import Document
from langchain.document_loaders import NotionDBLoader

integration_token = "your_notion_api_key"
database_id = "your_notion_database_id"
loader = NotionDBLoader(integration_token, database_id)
docs = loader.load()
```
