body: The problem is that callback handler events are not guaranteed to be non-blocking, for example, they can write to disk or db/make api calls, etc. At some point, we will restrict only using async callback handlers with the async callback manager but until then, we think it's best to run the callback in the event in an executor to avoid blocking the event loop. cc @nfcampos
author: agola11

body:
> we think it's best to run the callback in the event in an executor to avoid blocking the event loop

Sorry for missing that detail. I didn't understand that running in the executor with executor=None would actually run in a different thread. 

> At some point, we will restrict only using async callback handlers with the async callback manager

This is interesting direction.

1. I'm interested in why are you heading this direction. From what I understand, I can write an arbitrary blocking call in a function that's async, and langchain framework can't really know that. So that will force callback authors to have async interface, but inside the interface anything can happen, and you can't really protect users from themselves writing a blocking call inside async function. Is that better than just calling a plain function?


2. From the user perspective (thinking naively), does it mean that I won't be able to call both `generate` and `agenerate` on the same LLM object?

```py
llm = OpenAI(..., callbacks=[my_sync_callbacks])
llm.generate(...)
llm.agenerate(...)
```

Will one of them fail?


----

Why am I looking into it at all?

The main reason I've started looking into this is because I use https://opentelemetry.io/docs/instrumentation/python/ to instrument all LLM requests in the codebase. I think it's become a standard and it might be interesting to you. The setup is pretty much this
- on_llm_start we start a new span and set it as active (active span is kept in contextvar, all handled by open telemetry)
- on_llm_end we end the span.

That worked very well until https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/3256. The reason it stopped working is that each invocation of event handler function gets its own task now (as a consequence of calling gather [here](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/e0e3ef1c57109ac5491ba744b8e4a4189931b1b5/langchain/callbacks/manager.py#L254-L261)). The actual http call again happens in totally different task (and different contextvar context) again.

Their contextvar contexts look something like this. They all fork from the same context.


```mermaid
graph TD;
    root-->on_llm_start;
    root-->request_1;
    root-->request_n;
    root-->a("on_llm_end");
```

That means that, whatever span on_llm_start marks as "active", it won't matter because the task (and context) will be destroyed. That means that HTTP calls traced can't be assigned to a span started in `on_llm_start` because that context is destroyed, and not shared with requests and on_llm_end handler.

How that means is that if you want to have traces like (imagine first http request failed so we retried)

```py
| -------- llm_request -------|
  |http req 1| |http req 2|
```
you actually can't because no callback can actually set the top llm_request span that will be visible (through contextvars) to the request tasks.

One could think that it's too opentelemetry specific, but I think the issue with the current async hooks design is wider. Anything that uses contextvars and has to share some data between hooks and the requests (through contextvars) will hit the same problem.

We can certainly implement tracing by monkeypatching some methods (either langchain's or clients), but the documentation states that callbacks are useful for tracing and I wanted to expose this implementation issue with the team.

body:
And by adding verify option: you can finally pass in headers such as 

`headers = {'User-Agent': 'Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10_11_5) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/50.0.2661.102 Safari/537.36'}`

to bypass the SSL verification.

```
headers = {'User-Agent': 'Mozilla/5.0 (Macintosh; Intel Mac OS X 10_11_5) AppleWebKit/537.36 (KHTML, like Gecko) Chrome/50.0.2661.102 Safari/537.36'}
loader = WebBaseLoader(web_path="https://SO_AND_SO.com", header_template=headers, verify=False)
data = loader.load()
```

This solves a lot of issues that I faced in the recent past.

author: jeffchuber

body:
@rlancemartin Is it also the intention for this change to standardize the vector DB ids to be UUIDs? The documentation for the supabase integration ( [python](https://python.langchain.com/docs/modules/data_connection/vectorstores/integrations/supabase) / [javascript](https://js.langchain.com/docs/modules/data_connection/vectorstores/integrations/supabase) ) suggests BIGSERIAL as the type. The underlying database is PostgreSQL with a PGVector extension.

At this moment, if we call the add_documents method, it does not send a list of ids to add_texts method.
```python
base.py:
    def add_documents(self, documents: List[Document], **kwargs: Any) -> List[str]:
        """Run more documents through the embeddings and add to the vectorstore.

        Args:
            documents (List[Document]: Documents to add to the vectorstore.

        Returns:
            List[str]: List of IDs of the added texts.
        """
        # TODO: Handle the case where the user doesn't provide ids on the Collection
        texts = [doc.page_content for doc in documents]
        metadatas = [doc.metadata for doc in documents]
        return self.add_texts(texts, metadatas, **kwargs)

supabase.py:
    def add_texts(
        self,
        texts: Iterable[str],
        metadatas: Optional[List[dict[Any, Any]]] = None,
        ids: Optional[List[str]] = None,
        **kwargs: Any,
    ) -> List[str]:
        ids = ids or [str(uuid.uuid4()) for _ in texts]
        docs = self._texts_to_documents(texts, metadatas)

        vectors = self._embedding.embed_documents(list(texts))
        return self.add_vectors(vectors, docs, ids)
```

The add_texts method then creates UUIDs and the `upsert` will fail if the table was declared like so:
```sql
-- Create a table to store your documents
create table documents (
  id bigserial primary key,
  content text,
  metadata jsonb,
  embedding vector(1536)
);
```

Any recommendations here?
author: michaeltansg

body: Ah, I see. Will BIGSERIAL always be used as Supabase primary key? If so, we can modify ID. Feel free to put up a PR!

body:
Maybe also update this and make the newer models the default?

```
    def _get_encoding_model(self) -> Tuple[str, tiktoken.Encoding]:
        tiktoken_ = _import_tiktoken()
        model = self.model_name
        if model == "gpt-3.5-turbo":
            # gpt-3.5-turbo may change over time.
            # Returning num tokens assuming gpt-3.5-turbo-0301.
            model = "gpt-3.5-turbo-0301"
        elif model == "gpt-4":
            # gpt-4 may change over time.
            # Returning num tokens assuming gpt-4-0314.
            model = "gpt-4-0314"
```

author: vercel

body:
@rlancemartin 
> Thanks for contributing! Do you also mind updating the redis example notebook to show usage of delete records by ID? (Semi-related: if easy, it would be helpful to explain how to create / connect to redis cloud or local DB in the notebook. Notebook currently only mentioned connection via url, but doesn't add much context about setting up the DB itself.)
> 
> https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/docs/extras/modules/data_connection/vectorstores/integrations/redis.ipynb

I've added an example of the deletion of entries and how to create an instance with the classmethod `from_texts_return_keys` to get the keys while creating the instance itself.

To be honest I think it shouldn't be part of the notebook to explain how to setup the DB itself..
author: ChrKahl

body:
> @rlancemartin 
> 
> > Thanks for contributing! Do you also mind updating the redis example notebook to show usage of delete records by ID? (Semi-related: if easy, it would be helpful to explain how to create / connect to redis cloud or local DB in the notebook. Notebook currently only mentioned connection via url, but doesn't add much context about setting up the DB itself.)
> 
> > 
> 
> > https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/docs/extras/modules/data_connection/vectorstores/integrations/redis.ipynb
> 
> 
> 
> I've added an example of the deletion of entries and how to create an instance with the classmethod `from_texts_return_keys` to get the keys while creating the instance itself.
> 
> 
> 
> To be honest I think it shouldn't be part of the notebook to explain how to setup the DB itself..

Great!

Notebook should give context on the redis_url. (Is this a local instance? How to context to a cloud hosted DB? Etc.) Many of the other DBs provide that. See the Elastic documentation for example. But of course it's not on you to add it and is unrelated to this PR, so of course no worries if you do not want to do it. We can get this PR merged without it obviously.
author: rlancemartin

body: @ChrKahl I merged this, and made some small changes in a [follow-up](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/6126) (adding `delete` to several vectorDB). Please have a quick look to ensure you are OK with the changes. In particular, see this [line](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/6126/files#diff-62b1da831f64356cb6ae89ebc4f53e6f51c1db9842e450771af894743fc71eebR467), which ensure delete can accept `ids` or `keys` as the arg name (for consistency w/ the others, which use `ids`).

only two small things

1. not sure i see the 'force function_call' argument
https://github.com/jxnl/openai_function_call/blob/main/example_segmentation.py#L61

doing `function_call={"name": ...}` on `Completion.create`

the api to force the usage, maybe abstracted away and already there, not sure

https://github.com/jxnl/openai_function_call/blob/main/example_segmentation.py#L36-L42https://github.com/jxnl/openai_function_call/blob/main/example_segmentation.py#L36-L42

https://github.com/jxnl/openai_function_call/blob/main/openai_function_call.py#L63

body:
> this changes existing functionality right?

This PR should not change the existing functionality. It just changes the params for getting pages to expand the `restrictions` property. So we don't need the additional http requests to get the restrictions when checking if the page is public.

The tests fail because the mocked page content has no `restrictions` property.

Related API Reference: https://developer.atlassian.com/cloud/confluence/rest/v1/api-group-content/#api-wiki-rest-api-content-get

body:
Still can not pass a system message to model. here is my code:
```
llm = AzureChatOpenAI(
     deployment_name=OPENAI_DEPLOYMENT_NAME,
     model_name="gpt-3.5",
     temperature=0.0,
    
 )
python_agent = create_csv_agent(
                            llm,
                            './device_data.csv',
                            verbose=True,
                            agent_executor_kwargs={"handle_parsing_errors": True},
                             agent_type=AgentType.ZERO_SHOT_REACT_DESCRIPTION,
                      )

tools = [Tool(
                name="PythonREPL",
                func=python_agent.run,
                description="useful for when you need to run python code in a REPL to answer questions, for example for more complex calculations or other code executions necessary to be able to answer correctly. Input should be clear python code, nothing else. You should always use a final print() statement for the final result to be able to read the outputs."
                ),]

system_message = """you are a data analyst assistant
            
            [instructions for query answering]:
            In the Action Input section, always excute the Print function.
            In the Action Input section, don't convert the result to json format.
            
            If you find no data or Empty DataFrame, do not adjust the query,don't do any other thing that the query is not asking, 
            and reply 'There is no related data to answer your query'
            """

agent = initialize_agent(
          tools,
          llm,
          agent=AgentType.OPENAI_FUNCTIONS,
          system_message=system_message,
          agent_kwargs={
             "system_message": system_message
            },
      )
```
It is not working. 
what should I modify to make it work?

body:
Hi, I try to use my comany's token as api key for initializing AzureOpenAI, but it seems like token contains an invalid number of segments, have you encountered the same problem before? 

`# authenticate to Azure
credentials = ClientSecretCredential(const.IQVIA_TENANT_ID, const.SERVICE_PRINCIPAL, const.SERVICE_PRINCIPAL_SECRET)
token = credentials.get_token(const.SCOPE_NON_INTERACTIVE)
# access openai account
openai.api_type = "azure_ad"
openai.api_key = token.token
openai.api_base = f"{const.OPENAI_API_BASE}/{const.OPENAI_API_TYPE}/{const.OPENAI_ACCOUNT_NAME}"
openai.api_version = const.OPENAI_API_VERSION

llm = AzureOpenAI(deployment_name=dep.GPT_35_TURBO, openai_api_version=const.OPENAI_API_VERSION, openai_api_key=openai.api_key)
# Run the LLM
llm("Tell me a joke")`
openai.error.AuthenticationError: invalid token provided: token contains an invalid number of segments

A member of the Team first needs to [authorize it](https://vercel.com/git/authorize?team=LangChain&type=github&job=%7B%22headInfo%22%3A%7B%22sha%22%3A%224beda8446747475ad011093cf9a56459936c2cc0%22%7D%2C%22id%22%3A%22QmQpsBDdbdJvpEwwq1CKBap9kjshmRSwT8v4ykeXCKULAx%22%2C%22org%22%3A%22hwchase17%22%2C%22prId%22%3A6315%2C%22repo%22%3A%22langchain%22%7D).
author: vercel

body:
Thanks @vowelparrot! I have been working on this with Tim. On the high level feedback:

> It's explicitly targeting agents (or rather the agent executor state machine). That's great, I think, especially at first, but I wonder if we want to indicate this in any way via the callback naming since it doesn't support arbitrary "chain" or other workflows.

Yep, we mentioned that in the docstring and would want to call it out in the docs & examples we share too.
* Only concern about changing the name is that we DO want to make it more general later so would that be an annoying name change to deprecate / break in a later version?
* BTW, any ideas on what the right flow / UI would be for this in a non-agent Chain? (like a raw SQLChain or something?) We wracked our brains a bit and weren't totally sure.

> I may be misreading, but it doesn't seem like it supports at most one thought at a time, right? I think that's fine to start, just want to understand the limits a bit here.

I'm not sure I understand what you mean by this, could you share a little more detail or an example of what you mean? The callback integration should support one thought or arbitrarily many thoughts (as seen in the [example app](https://langchain-mrkl.streamlit.app/)). Although one thought / no tool use would render fine but be a bit awkward - related to the chain comment above - very open to ideas on how to support this.

Thank you!
author: sfc-gh-jcarroll

body:
Thanks for the review, @vowelparrot! I'll take care of the nits today, and get started on integration tests. 

I'm not able to add Streamlit as an optional dependency because our supported Python version range excludes Python 3.9.7 due to some issue we had with it, and Poetry won't resolve Streamlit because of that 3.9.7 exclusion.
author: tconkling

body:
@vowelparrot (and cc @sfc-gh-jcarroll) - I'm removing the draft status from this PR, and would love another review if you have a chance.

Some notes:
- There's a few unit tests for the "auto-upgrading callback handler" feature that pulls StreamlitCallbackHandler from the streamlit package itself, if it has a newer version of the callback handler
- There's a very basic integration test for the handler. It just runs the "Who is Olivia Wilde's boyfriend" agent query that I saw used in other tests. Obviously, this test requires network access, but:
  - I wrote a (very simple!) tool for testing CallbackHandlers that saves an Agent's callback invocations to a pickle file, and lets you "replay" those callbacks against any set of CallbackHandlers without hitting the network (or spending your token budget). If this would be useful, I'd be happy to add it to the PR and use it in the integration test. (It's just a single small Python file.)
- Re: naming - happy to rename this to `StreamlitAgentCallbackHandler` or whatever you think is appropriate; just let me know and I'll push the change.

Thanks!
author: tconkling

body: Will review in an hour!
author: vowelparrot

body:
@sfc-gh-jcarroll I think this looks good as is, and with the current name. 

In terms of other non-agent flows that are common - I'd say:
-  Some fixed retrieval -> response synthesis for Q&A or summarization, so map reduce chain, SQL DB chain 
- Information extraction
- Some of the experimental agents (BabyAGI, Generative agents, etc.)

You know your users better than I, so maybe the first two are less important for visualizing in streamlit. Perhaps verifying this works reasonably well with custom agents that don't use the existing agent executor would be good, even if it doesn't impact this PR directly
author: vowelparrot

body:
Awesome!! Anything else needed on our end before this can be merged and released?

We will give those other workflows a try and see what viz makes sense with this callback handler in Streamlit (future work). Thank you!!
author: sfc-gh-jcarroll

author: vercel

body:
This score implementation affects similarity_search_with_relevance_scores(base.py).
In _similarity_search_with_relevance_scores(base.py), score value change by relevance_score_fn and check scores. faiss.py and base.py uses same 'score_threshold' value.

In similarity_search_with_score_by_vector(faiss.py), original score value is checked by 'score_threshold'.
But in similarity_search_with_relevance_scores(base.py), score value changed by relevance_score_fn is checked by the same 'score_threshold'.

So, if call similarity_search_with_relevance_scores, score value filtering is processed twice on different criteria before and after conversion.
author: tak-s

body:
> This score implementation affects similarity_search_with_relevance_scores(base.py). In _similarity_search_with_relevance_scores(base.py), score value change by relevance_score_fn and check scores. faiss.py and base.py uses same 'score_threshold' value.
> 
> In similarity_search_with_score_by_vector(faiss.py), original score value is checked by 'score_threshold'. But in similarity_search_with_relevance_scores(base.py), score value changed by relevance_score_fn is checked by the same 'score_threshold'.
> 
> So, if call similarity_search_with_relevance_scores, score value filtering is processed twice on different criteria before and after conversion.

This was fixed by #6570. Thanks.
author: tak-s

I think I got a correct test written and it's passing.

author: thehunmonkgroup

body:
> > wont we want to include additional_kwargs?
> 
> @hwchase17 none of the other elements of that function add `additional_kwargs` besides the `function_call` in the `AIMessage` conversion. It's not clear to me what we would even add to `additional_kwargs` in the `FunctionMessage` case.
> 
> Here's the example code from OpenAI, looks like passing a function message is just the `role`, `name`, and `content` to me:
> 
> ```json
> {
>   "model": "gpt-3.5-turbo-0613",
>   "messages": [
>     {"role": "user", "content": "What is the weather like in Boston?"},
>     {"role": "assistant", "content": null, "function_call": {"name": "get_current_weather", "arguments": "{ \"location\": \"Boston, MA\"}"}},
>     {"role": "function", "name": "get_current_weather", "content": "{\"temperature\": "22", \"unit\": \"celsius\", \"description\": \"Sunny\"}"}
>   ],
>   "functions": [
>     {
>       "name": "get_current_weather",
>       "description": "Get the current weather in a given location",
>       "parameters": {
>         "type": "object",
>         "properties": {
>           "location": {
>             "type": "string",
>             "description": "The city and state, e.g. San Francisco, CA"
>           },
>           "unit": {
>             "type": "string",
>             "enum": ["celsius", "fahrenheit"]
>           }
>         },
>         "required": ["location"]
>       }
>     }
>   ]
> }
> ```
> 
> If you could confirm that the existing code is correct, I'll try to work up a unit test, first time contributor, so I'll need to fumble my way through it ;)

ah right sorry, got confused

thanks!

author: davidbuniat

body:
I just encountered this with version 3.6.5. Version check has not been updated, and is inconsistent as well. I.e., in deeplake.py, 127-132:

```
version = deeplake.__version__
if version != "3.6.2":
       raise ValueError(
            "deeplake version should be = 3.6.3, but you've installed"
             f" {version}. Consider changing deeplake version to 3.6.3 ."
        )
```
(This check will fail if version is 3.6.3!)

Suggestion (tested on my local installation):

1) define minimum_version globally, e.g., about line 23:

```
minimum_version = "3.6.3"
```

2) Modify version check to:

```
version = deeplake.__version__
if not version >= minimum_version:      
       raise ValueError(
            f"deeplake version should be >= {minimum_version}, but you've installed"
            f" {version}. Consider changing deeplake version to {minimum_version} or later ."
        )
```

3) Update one line of code when updating version, if necessary.

body:
Consider using the `-v|vv|vvv` option to gather more information, maybe it's a network issue.

Additionally, if you are using a generator, the type annotation for `get_snippets()` should be `Generator[str, None, None]`. Don't forget to update the `results()` function to use the latest API.

author: vercel

body:
> why cant you just pass in `ids` using the keys variable? they seem pretty overlapping in functionality?

Thanks for the comments!. Not sure if I understood correctly, but if you meant to let users define `ids` inside `keys` parameter, I don't think users should care about the Redis key semantics. Instead, the semantics of `ids` remain as just identifiers instead of governing the index scheme inside Redis. 

Redis keys are generated [here](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/22601b0b6323e6465f78ca9bc16152062a2b65ba/langchain/vectorstores/redis.py#L78) whose prefix is generated [here](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/22601b0b6323e6465f78ca9bc16152062a2b65ba/langchain/vectorstores/redis.py#L85). In Redis, those prefixes or namespaces are used as a primary index key and users should not arbitrarily define it without understanding those. For example, users can define keys not conforming [the index defined in the Redis object](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/langchain/vectorstores/redis.py#L134). I argue custom IDs should not affect any indexing scheme.

Among many others, AnalyticDB has [`ids` argument](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/22601b0b6323e6465f78ca9bc16152062a2b65ba/langchain/vectorstores/analyticdb.py#L128) that users can arbitrarily define.

In fact, I think the best solution for this is for langchain to hide `keys` concept at Redis because namespaces, which are indices in langchain, should be considered to be databases or tables in traditional databases, and custom IDs should be agnostic to those (which should be an ID to an entry in a table, etc.)

I don't know the policy to make a breaking change, so I allowed both `keys` and `ids` but I strongly believe `keys` should not be exposed in the functions.
author: jbkoh

body: @DvirDukhan a kind ping in case you missed the alarm... thanks!
author: jbkoh

body:
@jbkoh thanks
I have read your PR. IIUC, you want to keep whatever namespacing you get from langchain concerning the key prefix but maintain your ID suffix. Did I get it right?
author: DvirDukhan

body:
Thanks for the reply! 

Correct. Because we define "index" per Redis instance, I'd argue index-specific prefixes should be abstracted and the end users should rely only on ids (suffixes of the keys). I shouldn't be able to add a key whose index (prefix) is different from the one defined within the scope of the instance.
author: jbkoh

body:
I see that master branch has introduced "keys_or_ids" concepts here: https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/commit/be02572d586bcb33fffe89c37b81d5ba26762bec#diff-62b1da831f64356cb6ae89ebc4f53e6f51c1db9842e450771af894743fc71eebR213
but I think keys and ids are semantically different.
author: jbkoh

body: @jbkoh is this still relevant for you? Can you not just override the key name with Id's? am I missing something here or can we close this?
author: Spartee

body:
> @jbkoh is this still relevant for you? Can you not just override the key name with Id's? am I missing something here or can we close this?

Hi @Spartee. I understand we can override the keys, but I was arguing in this PR that we should abstract away the key structure of the Redis. IDs should only mean the customizable part of the key while the whole key exposes the indexing structure in Redis which should not be controlled at this API.
author: jbkoh

body:
I do see what you mean. But I disagree that the indexing should be at that level of abstraction. Provided some concrete examples in which that is of some benefit, I'm happy to reconsider. 

Tagging @baskaryan for his opinion
author: Spartee

body:
I'm lazy to write actual code for now but here are a few immediate cases
- The ID structures are different from other vector store interfaces in LangChain, and it reduces the compatibility with other vector stores while LangChain is supposed to standardize them all.
  - From Redis's customer management perspective, I guess it'll increase the bar to adopt LangChain compared to the others.
- I want to index each page of PDF and want to update vector each when I change the embedding scheme. I don't know the key structure (I mean as a human I know it, but the code structure is not supposed to expose the key structure in the current LangChain flow.) Basically, a controlled ID per PDF/page#.
author: jbkoh

body:
@jbkoh Hi , could you, please, resolve the merging issues and address the last comments (if needed)? After that, ping me and I push this PR for the review. Thanks!
If this PR is not needed anymore, could you, please, let me know or close this PR?
author: leo-gan

body: @leo-gan I'm more of waiting for a guidance (go/no-no) given it "could" be a breaking change to the the convention of IDs specific to  langchain-redis users. A few in this thread shared the concerns so I'd like to be on the right direction before putting more effort on the coding?
author: jbkoh

body: @baskaryan FYI ^
author: leo-gan

body:
I believe this landed as part of #8612 and #11257 ! 

Closing this because the PR wouldn't line up with the current directory structure of the library (would need to be in /libs/langchain/langchain instead of /langchain). Feel free to reopen against the current head if there's parts of the way keys/ids are used that I'm missing!

In general, we try to avoid breaking changes, so anything that breaks existing functionality would need a very compelling reason to do so.

author: ruoccofabrizio

body:
> @CameronVetter integrated in #6843 relying only on the metadata dictionary for additional fields. It supports also custom index creation directly within LangChain.

I'm not sure I agree with copy/pasting this bug fix into a larger feature PR when this valid and simple PR is ready to merge. It would have been better to get this fix in quickly for the people waiting for it when it was ready last week...  I suppose as long as it gets into the code base ASAP...
author: CameronVetter

body:
> > @CameronVetter integrated in #6843 relying only on the metadata dictionary for additional fields. It supports also custom index creation directly within LangChain.
> 
> I'm not sure I agree with copy/pasting this bug fix into a larger feature PR when this valid and simple PR is ready to merge. It would have been better to get this fix in quickly for the people waiting for it when it was ready last week... I suppose as long as it gets into the code base ASAP...

First, this is not a bug fix. Second, this is not copy/pasting as you are using an additional field instead of relying on the metadata dictionary which is already part of the standard LangChain document structure. Also, not giving the ability to add extra fields in the index definition as per this PR creates the need to work outside of the framework to add additional fields, which is not ideal.
author: ruoccofabrizio

body:
> > > @CameronVetter integrated in #6843 relying only on the metadata dictionary for additional fields. It supports also custom index creation directly within LangChain.
> > 
> > 
> > I'm not sure I agree with copy/pasting this bug fix into a larger feature PR when this valid and simple PR is ready to merge. It would have been better to get this fix in quickly for the people waiting for it when it was ready last week... I suppose as long as it gets into the code base ASAP...
> 
> First, this is not a bug fix. Second, this is not copy/pasting as you are using an additional field instead of relying on the metadata dictionary which is already part of the standard LangChain document structure. Also, not giving the ability to add extra fields in the index definition as per this PR creates the need to work outside of the framework to add additional fields, which is not ideal.

Looking at the overall code structure it sure seems like a lot of the design for this was intended functionality that was missed in the original implementation, thus my calling it a bug.  I could see the argument for it being though, I initially didn't classify it as a bug and only thought of it as one later.

The creation of the index as a side effect of trying to use a vector store that is not there is not desirable, and runs against the pattern of all of the other vector store implementations within langchain, so I'm not sure that adding even more to that is going in the right direction.  I think it would be better to remove that completely and instead give examples of how to create the example and follow already established patterns.

Agreed, using metadata is an improvement.  In the spirit  of getting this merged I'm closing this.  See #6843
author: CameronVetter

body:
Any news on that #6843 @CameronVetter @ruoccofabrizio ???

we would really love this #6843 to be merge @hwchase17

body: Azure metadata does not support multi-level index field, thus we have to populate the fields with separate ones rather than using a single "metadata" field. This whole thing needs to be revamped.

body:
> @rlancemartin we have the concept of parsers - how does this relate to that?

AFAIK parsers are used in LangChain to process the output of a model response and convert it to a Python struct.

In the context of this PR, parser refers to the manipulation of a source code written in a programming language, in order to separate chunks of text in a meaningful way.

Any suggestion about naming is welcome :smile:
author: cristobalcl

body:
> @rlancemartin we have the concept of parsers - how does this relate to that?

@hwchase17 you're referring to BlobParsers here, right (not OutputParser)

body:
Nice work! Interesting PR. 

Suggestion: we can re-organize the code slightly to fit into a `loader` -> `parser` workflow as we do [here](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/docs/extras/modules/data_connection/document_loaders/integrations/youtube_audio.ipynb).

(1) You should be able to use existing `FileSystemBlobLoader` [here](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/langchain/document_loaders/blob_loaders/file_system.py) to load the .js or .py files. No new code should be required for loading.

(2) Then, just move your parsing logic into a new parser file in the directory [here](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/tree/master/langchain/document_loaders/parsers).

The UX is simple and we reduce code duplication (no new loading code). Specifically, the UX can follow what we do [here](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/docs/extras/modules/data_connection/document_loaders/integrations/youtube_audio.ipynb) where the loader and parser are called:

```
# Transcribe the videos to text
loader = GenericLoader(YoutubeAudioLoader(urls, save_dir), OpenAIWhisperParser())
docs = loader.load()
```

In this case, of course it will look like:
```
# Parse js or py to docs
loader = GenericLoader(FileSystemBlobLoader(path="",glob=""), <New_Code_Parser>())
docs = loader.load()
```

Thoughts?
author: rlancemartin

body:
Oh, right! That makes much more sense. I wasn't familiar with GenericLoader, and I wasn't sure how parsers were used. Now that's clear.

Let me refactor the code.
author: cristobalcl

body:
Done! It's much better now, in my opinion.

One thing to note: I've reused `langchain.text_splitter.Language` to avoid duplicate things, but maybe that class should be moved to a global module.

More suggestions are welcome!
author: cristobalcl

body:
> Done! It's much better now, in my opinion.
> 
> One thing to note: I've reused `langchain.text_splitter.Language` to avoid duplicate things, but maybe that class should be moved to a global module.
> 
> More suggestions are welcome!

Nice! Yes, clean UX:

```
loader = GenericLoader.from_filesystem(
    "./example_data/languages",
    glob="*",
    suffixes=[".py", ".js"],
    parser=LanguageParser()
)
docs = loader.load()
```

Small clarification in the docs: 

1) it will default split on classes and functions? can this be configured (e.g., only split on functions)?
2) will the `top-level code, but without the already loaded functions and classes` doc always be generated?

This is looking good. I think we only need to clarify usage a bit. 

In short, it simply looks like this will by default split any js or py based on class and function definitions?
author: rlancemartin

body:
Exactly, it only splits, or segments, the source code based on top-level classes and functions, and the remaining code.

I've edited the documentation trying to make it more straightforward. Also, the docstring mentions the only parameters that can be configured: `language` and `parser_threshold`.

Should I add an example in the notebook like the one I mention in the first post in this PR? I thought that the code and the results would be self-explanatory, but perhaps it's not clear enough.
author: cristobalcl

body:
Can we add this to the base VectorStore class or add a specialized subclass? It seems FAISS already has this implemented: https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/langchain/vectorstores/faiss.py#L81. Happy to open a PR with this change if we decide to move this to base class! 

Also potentially related PR: https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/6564/files as the diffrenet distance metrics will affect how the score threshold filters results.
author: raymond-yuan

body:
This includes some of the changes you've included in this PR, do you want to take a look? Happy to collaborate/merge your additional changes in if we think it's worth refactoring in this way:

https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/6570
author: raymond-yuan

body:
Hi @raymond-yuan, the metric scores may differ between the databases, for example, pgvector returns the negative of maximum inner product while chroma will return a positive value for this metric, so the normalization/ scaling function should match the database.
I think the default **relevance_score_fn** should be left unimplemented in base for the specific vector store to implement. Besides that I think it is a necessary refactor and a great job in #6570.
I have two pull requests that include related code - this one and #6475. Those versions are working well and the normalization functions I added for chroma and pgvector are tested.
You want to take my chroma and pgvector normalization functions to your pull request and I will resolve the conflicts and match my PRs to the new code? Or the other way around when I merge my changes and the refactor will be done after?
Let me know.
author: hanit-com

body:
>  default relevance_score_fn should be left unimplemented in base

~~I think this should be fine right? We let the vector store define whatever relevance score in `_select_relevance_score_fn` and we rely on that function in `_similarity_search_with_relevance_scores` or maybe I'm misunderstanding your point?~~

gotcha, will revert those changes - i think euclidean and cosine should be standard across all? wdyt
edit: actually, i think we should keep it in the base class- these should be standard across vector stores - instead we can do somehting like:
```
    def _max_inner_product_relevance_score_fn(distance: float) -> float:
        """Normalize the distance to a score on a scale [0, 1]."""
        if distance > 0:
            return 1.0 - distance
    
        return -1.0 * distance
```
wdyt to handle score values 

> You want to take my chroma and pgvector normalization functions to your pull request and I will resolve the conflicts and match my PRs to the new code? Or the other way around when I merge my changes and the refactor will be done after? Let me know.

Either way works! I think maybe the easiest approach would be to merge your changes in to my PR since it is a bit of a superset of changes? Let me know if you want me to go ahead and merge the two PRs into mine
author: raymond-yuan

body:
I like this suggestion, nevertheless, each vector DB returns a different formula, there might be vectorstore that will return the inner product already normalized or return (1.0 - max_inner product). It is a broad subject to resolve in one pull request so I think it is better to handle it in vector store level while keeping each vector store's normalization function as it was.
The centralized normalization functions solution can work if you are confident about it, and it might require adjusting the tests to match the new scores (if changed).
It is also a good thing the user can provide his own implementation in case we got it inaccurately.
About the pull requests, if it works for you then sure, you can merge them into yours!
author: hanit-com

body:
I think it's good to provide some default functions because most of the implementations are standardized. The current implementation does not prevent users from specifying different relevance functions for specific vectorbases, for example one could directly pass the score through the _select_relevance_score_fn without normalizing it if the db has already done this. As of now all of the normalization functions work with the tests as is. We could potentially move the centralized functions into vectorstores/utils, but in either case because so many implementations rely on relatively standard functions, i think providing some default functions for the relevance functions helps a lot with code duplication. 

I went ahead and merged your changes in to my PR (and handled conflicts)!
author: raymond-yuan

body: Great! thank you @raymond-yuan
author: hanit-com

body:
Thanks! Can you please rebase on `master` to resolve merge conflicts?

You can update `pyproject.toml` and then just `checkout master poetry.lock`. 

Then, re-generate poetry.lock with:
```
poetry lock --no-update
```
author: rlancemartin

body: @kdcokenny do you mind rebasing or merging `master` to resolve conflicts?
author: rlancemartin

body:
> @kdcokenny do you mind rebasing or merging `master` to resolve conflicts?

Yeah, no problem
author: kdcokenny

body:
> > @kdcokenny do you mind rebasing or merging `master` to resolve conflicts?
> 
> Yeah, no problem

@kdcokenny one thing to check: `UnstructuredRSTLoader` recently [went in](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/6594) and produces some merge conflicts. Does `PandocRSTLoader ` have capabilities in addition to that? If so, we might simply add it to the existing `rst` notebook?
author: rlancemartin

body:
@kdcokenny appreciate the work here. 

Given https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/6594, is there anything from this PR that we want to add that extends the functionality or clarity?

body:
@ofermend can you please have a look at the Lint errors - 
```
langchain/vectorstores/vectara.py:153: error: Argument "files" to "post" of "Session" has incompatible type "Dict[str, Sequence[Iterable[Sequence[object]]]]"; expected "Optional[Union[Mapping[str, Union[Union[SupportsRead[Union[str, bytes]], str, bytes], Tuple[Optional[str], Union[SupportsRead[Union[str, bytes]], str, bytes]], Tuple[Optional[str], Union[SupportsRead[Union[str, bytes]], str, bytes], str], Tuple[Optional[str], Union[SupportsRead[Union[str, bytes]], str, bytes], str, Mapping[str, str]]]], Iterable[Tuple[str, Union[Union[SupportsRead[Union[str, bytes]], str, bytes], Tuple[Optional[str], Union[SupportsRead[Union[str, bytes]], str, bytes]], Tuple[Optional[str], Union[SupportsRead[Union[str, bytes]], str, bytes], str], Tuple[Optional[str], Union[SupportsRead[Union[str, bytes]], str, bytes], str, Mapping[str, str]]]]]]]"  [arg-type]
langchain/vectorstores/vectara.py:194: error: Unsupported target for indexed assignment ("Optional[Dict[Any, Any]]")  [index]
```

We can get this in once those are resolved.
author: rlancemartin

body:
@arunchinnachamy 
> the parameter needs to be removed from Line No. 188 as well in the function scrape.

Line 188 is using `self.session` which is a `requests` [session object](https://requests.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user/advanced/#ssl-cert-verification)
I've refactored the code and moved this param setting in the constructor

body: we had it like this before but got feedback from other folks that ecosystem was a confusing grouping. maybe we wait to gather more feedback?
author: dev2049

body:
> we had it like this before but got feedback from other folks that ecosystem was a confusing grouping. maybe we wait to gather more feedback?

I agree that "Ecosystem" is a too broad term and hence confusing. 
Still, [PyTorch uses this term](https://pytorch.org/ecosystem/). 

This PR is a trade-off between moving "Additional resources" items into "Ecosystem" or opposite, moving "Ecosystem" items into "Additional resources". The latter doesn't work because "YouTube" and "Gallery" can be part of the "Ecosystem" but the "Integrations" cannot be part of the "Additional resources". So, decision was to move "YouTube" and "Gallery" into the "Ecosystem". 

How about renaming "Ecosystem" to "Resources" (as [in PyTorch again](https://pytorch.org/resources/))? 

BTW @hwchase17 insisted on "Ecosystem" vs "Integrations" the last time.

body: @raymond-yuan I see tests for chroma, faiss, pgvector, singlestore. but a larger set of DBs (pinecone, etc) are modified; tests for some like pinecone are tricky b/c they are not in memory, but do we have confirmation (e.g., from user testing) that this is a no-op / backwards compatible across the broader set of DBs? i see the the existing logic is moved to `_select_relevance_score_fn`, so it **should** be, but would be nice to verify it we could (e.g., a subtle change in scoring may confuse folks).
author: rlancemartin

body:
> @raymond-yuan I see tests for chroma, faiss, pgvector, singlestore. but a larger set of DBs (pinecone, etc) are modified; tests for some like pinecone are tricky b/c they are not in memory, but do we have confirmation (e.g., from user testing) that this is a no-op / backwards compatible across the broader set of DBs? i see the the existing logic is moved to `_select_relevance_score_fn`, so it **should** be, but would be nice to verify it we could (e.g., a subtle change in scoring may confuse folks).

@rlancemartin added some additional tests (using personal api keys), for additional dbs. To be clear, for vectorstores that already had the relevancy score function already implemented, these should be no-ops and completely backwards compatible. However for the majority of classes that did not have these implemented, the `_similarity_search_with_relevance_scores` will produce different results, whch is the desired outcome since these were incorrectly implemented before (since the old behavior was just doing a similarity search and returning the corresponding distance).
author: raymond-yuan

body:
> relevancy score function already implemented, these should be no-ops and completely backwards compatible

Ya, agreed. Cool, thanks for running some additional tests.

Going to merge this now, esp given the demand to get it in.

We can take up follow-up work on this in newer PRs.

Good work!
author: rlancemartin

body: @raymond-yuan Looking into this for the supabase retriever. Awesome work! Any reason why you left supabase out of this? Was it implemented correctly?
author: ShantanuNair

body: @ShantanuNair ah sorry, no I missed this because it already had a custom implementation of [`similarity_search_with_relevance_score`](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/langchain/vectorstores/supabase.py#L138). Upon closer inspection, I believe this is correctly implemented for the default `match_document` being used [documentation](https://supabase.com/blog/openai-embeddings-postgres-vector). This code already converts the default distance fn (cosine distance) into a similarity score `(1 - cos(x))`
author: raymond-yuan

body: Looks like relevant issue with Pinecone Cosine Similarity, which is converted back to distance - filtering out relevant documents when using `score_threshold` -- #8207
author: olegshirokikh

body:
I found the `score_threshold` also filters out the most relevant doc for the FAISS vectorstore. I think there are still bugs with this refactoring changes. `

First, the `FAISS.similarity_search_with_score` /`similarity_search_with_score_by_vector`  actually returns the score which already represents the similarity score instead of distance... (I validated with the `embed_function` with the same sentences and the dot product is 1 which matches the score from the function) but somehow the docstring says the lower score means more similarity? 

Second, the `_max_inner_product_relevance_score_fn`  should just return the score itself for FAISS (at least) if the higher score from `FAISS.similarity_search_with_score` /`similarity_search_with_score_by_vector` represents the higher similarity... let me know if I miss anything.   

I guess a fix is to implement the `_max_inner_product_relevance_score_fn` in the `FAISS` vectorstore file, like `lambda x: x`.

body:
Additionally, i think it is necessary to allow the "deployment" parameter to be optional:
https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/96dcc798d2f83b44f4ff9e886d1d6ecd743ded39/langchain/embeddings/openai.py#L110

https://github.com/openai/openai-python/blob/b82a3f7e4c462a8a10fa445193301a3cefef9a4a/openai/api_resources/abstract/engine_api_resource.py#L54

author: blob42

body:
Hey @blob42 ! Sorry nobody from the team commented on this sooner. From my understanding of what this PR does, this would be better achieved using a `RunnableBranch` https://python.langchain.com/docs/expression_language/how_to/routing#using-a-runnablebranch

This implements the same kind of logic as here in a generalizable way, which will select the output parser (or other runnable) that ultimately gets called during a Runnable's execution.

Let me know what you think! I'll probably close this later this week if it's ok with you because this PR doesn't seem very active anymore :)
author: efriis

body: @efriis Yes `RunnableBranch` seems to cover this use case. I started this PR before the feature was available. Feel free to close the PR.

body:
> i think probably not ideal to have every document loader inherit from this, maybe we can make a mixin though? only for document laoders which actually have a query param

If the document loader doesn't explicitly define the `query` parameter, it defaults to `everything`. If some document loaders also defined `query`, as `MaxComputeLoader` did, this `query` has the same semantics, so no braking change.
That's the idea, to allow every document loader to inherit the retriever functions.

body: One added point: currently any `parser` accept blobs and inherits from `BaseBlobParser`. In this case, it seemed easier to move your parser code into a new text splitter rather than modify your loader to yield blobs (since you do some processing and produce XML data). Open to feedback from others and will discuss w/ @eyurtsev when he is back from travels. IMO this is something we need to clarify in the documentation (specifically, differences between `loaders -> splitters` vs `blob loaders -> blob parsers`).
author: rlancemartin

body:
I went with option (1) mentioned in code above.

use a existing blob loader to load PDFs and add all the logic (XML generation) to the globid blob parser.

pro: 
+ simplifies the code by consolidating all logic into the parser (rather than separated btwn loader+parser)
+ re-uses existing blog loader for flexibility 
+ avoids adding bloat in text_splitter
+ UX is consistent w/ [recent context aware splitters](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/6486) 

UX:
```
loader = GenericLoader.from_filesystem(
    "./example_data/source_code",
    glob="*",
    suffixes=[".pdf"],
    parser= GrobidParser(segment_sentences=True)
)
docs = loader.load()
```

will add better documentation to help contributors recognize this.

body: When I use the gpt-3.5-turbo-16k model, I often encounter this ValueError 'A single document was so long it could not be combined' due to the small token_max.I always need to modify a larger value for token_max to resolve the issue.I hope to enhance the functionality by dynamically modifying the value of token_max through obtaining the model used by the current chain.
author: llmadd

body: We need a more dynamic way of obtaining the model name in order to retrieve the maximum tokens of the model. This aspect still requires further improvement.
author: llmadd

body:
thanks for the pr!

not quite sure i understand, can't you pass in the desired max token value already? how does changing the function default value help?

could you maybe share a code snippet for what you're trying to achieve?
author: dev2049

body:
```
chat = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-3.5-turbo-16k",temperature=0,callbacks=[StreamingStdOutCallbackHandler()])
prompt_template = """
下面以一篇文章的局部内容，你要为这个局部内容做个不损失重要信息的局部思维导图
这个思维导图要内容层次分明，能突出当前内容的主要信息
以markdown的格式输出答案
-----------------
{text}
-----------------
"""


PROMPT = PromptTemplate(template=prompt_template, input_variables=["text"])
combine_prompt_template = """
下面是一篇文章思维导图，你需要将这些信息进行汇总重构，生成一份新的格式优美的思维导图
要求新的思维导图，内容之间的关联性做到层次丰富，结构合理，不丢失重要信息，去除重复信息
以markdown的格式输出答案
-------
{text}
-------
"""
combine_prompt = PromptTemplate(
            input_variables=["text"],
            template=combine_prompt_template,)
chain_one = load_summarize_chain(chat, chain_type="map_reduce",return_intermediate_steps=True,verbose=True, map_prompt=PROMPT, combine_prompt=combine_prompt)
```


In this code snippet, I utilized mp-reduce to generate a mind map for an article. During the map phase, there were two instances where the token output from a large model exceeded 3000, triggering a ValueError 'A single document was so long it could not be combined'. I believe that when using the gpt-3.5-turbo-16k model, it would be more reasonable to have a larger token_max value instead of 3000. I resolved this error by making the necessary modifications, but it is specific to the gpt-3.5-turbo-16k model. Different models may require different values for token_max.

author: Lukalukaxxx

body:
Hey @Lukalukaxxx, it's great to see the minimax llm model to be integrated with Langchain. However, I didn't notice that this PR is in review, so I created a PR https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/7645 for the purpose as well. 

After taking a look, I found all the functionality in https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/7645 has been covered in this PR, but I'd like to discuss whether it would be better if we extract the calling API logic into an independent class, e.g. https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/7645/files#diff-0a9ce10f1093aede74365e42383221438316c05f618317f1c7b61b1614223ebcR25-R54? 

It would be genuinely appreciated if u could take a look at this comment!

cc: @baskaryan
author: hetaoBackend

author: nirga

body:
hm this is an interesting problem. i guess one alternative approach is you could have the top level chain's memory store the memory vars for all the sub-chains? so that you only have one place where you're storing state instead of `n`. but then you also have to think about each subchain not having overlapping memory variables

@hwchase17 any thoughts?
author: baskaryan

body:
> hm this is an interesting problem. i guess one alternative approach is you could have the top level chain's memory store the memory vars for all the sub-chains? so that you only have one place where you're storing state instead of `n`. but then you also have to think about each subchain not having overlapping memory variables
> 
> @hwchase17 any thoughts?

discussed internally, not obvious which approach is better so happy to enable both for now. thanks @nirga!

author: hemidactylus

body:
> Thank you for taking action @rlancemartin ! However, I am afraid an unresolved `<<<<<<< HEAD` resulted in the `poetry.lock` (from the force-push) which is what is making all tests above fail.

Ya, re-generating it now from the updated `pyproject.toml` :)

Just discussed a bit w/ @dev2049 and typical workflow when the `pyproject.toml` is updated is to run:

```
poetry lock --no-update
```

Which will re-generate the lock file (without updating the dependencies to their latest versions).

body:
This last commit adapts the Cassandra Vector Store to the newest cassIO library, which introduces a few improvements (including interface changes). Also the `delete` method is implemented.
Moreover, an important non-deterministic source of trouble is identified and healed in the associated integration test (related to the MMR search test). There, test coverage is also extended to the `delete` methods.

Sorry about the `poetry.lock` conflict :(
author: hemidactylus

body: @rlancemartin could you take a look at this Cassandra followup?
author: jbellis

body:
Thanks! Same as [6671](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/6771), can you please rebase on `master`, resolve merge conflict in `pyproject.toml` and then re-generate `poetry.lock` with `poetry lock --no-update`? I can also take this (I'm already doing it for the other PR). 

One other note: are there any conflicts between changes here and 6771 that we can resolve early (e.g., maybe they could be tested together by stacking the PRs).

author: homanp

body:
Great work! 

Just a quick question, does this PR help in my use case?

Due to the regulations in my company, when calling OpenAI, we need to 1 extra parameter. For example, I have to do this:

```python
import openai

openai.ChatCompletion.create(
    # ....setup model and start
    user: "user-id"
)
```

How to pass this extra "user" through? Thanks 😊
author: Albert-Gao

body:
> Great work!
> 
> Just a quick question, does this PR help in my use case?
> 
> Due to the regulations in my company, when calling OpenAI, we need to 1 extra parameter. For example, I have to do this:

Are you using LangChain? Seems your running vanilla OpenAI?

body: this is awesome @yarikoptic! looks like the new codespell CI check is failing :') could we add a make command or script to make it easy to run codespell locally?
author: baskaryan

body:
> this is awesome @yarikoptic! looks like the new codespell CI check is failing :')

someone was too active in master while having no codespell I see ;)   I have now

- discarded prior run of codespell commit 
- adjusted config to ignore more  (see recent commits for details)
- rebased on top of master
- reran codespell

so should be all green if ran/considered now! ;)

> could we add a make command or script to make it easy to run codespell locally?

need more *input*, since I bet you do not really need a script like

```bash
#!/bin/bash
codespell "$@"
```
right? ;)  the point is that running `codespell` is sufficient if you have it installed "properly" (given that config is in pyproject.toml you need `tomli` installed too).
author: yarikoptic

body:
> right? ;) the point is that running `codespell` is sufficient if you have it installed "properly" (given that config is in pyproject.toml you need `tomli` installed too).

haha fair, but i think probably still helpful to have simple make command for other contributors. added to Makefile and contributor guidelines, if that looks ok to you happy to land!

body:
> Distance-based vector database retrieval embeds (represents) queries in high-dimensional space and finds similar embedded documents based on "distance". But, retrieval may produce difference results with subtle changes in query wording or if the embeddings do not capture the semantics of the data well. Prompt engineering / tuning is sometimes done to manually address these problems, but can be tedious.
> 
> The `MultiQueryRetriever` automates the process of prompt tuning by using an LLM to generate multiple queries from different perspectives for a given user input query. For each query, it retrieves a set of relevant documents and takes the unique union across all queries to get a larger set of potentially relevant documents. By generating multiple perspectives on the same question, the `MultiQueryRetriever` might be able to overcome some of the limitations of the distance-based retrieval and get a richer set of results.

@rlancemartin great idea, thanks! Is it possible to combine this with ConversationalRetrievalChain by replacing question_generator prompt with yours that generates multiple questions based on user's question and chat history?

author: pablocastro

body:
> self.relevance_score_fn is relevant for _similarity_search_with_relevance_scores

Hi @hwchase17 , as I agree that self.relevance_score_fn is majorly used for _similarity_search_with_relevance_scores function but it is also effecting this stage as well. 

Consider the following scenario where we have a vectorstore built out of FAISS vectorstore: 

1. We get the retriever as retriever = vectorstore.as_retriever() which is mostly used for conversational chains. 
2. Given that the search type is set to "similarity_score_threshold", when we call retriever.get_relevant_documents(query) by setting the score threshold to say 0.8, the following workflow is triggered chronologically. (Let us keep in mind that 0.8 threshold set here is the threshold set on the basis of a score of 0 to 1 which ideally is the output of relevance_score_fn.

The steps called are: 

-      self.vectorstore.similarity_search_with_relevance_scores is triggered with the threshold of 0.8 (Line 410 File langchain.vectorstore.base)
-      The next step involves calling self._similarity_search_with_relevance_scores (Line 156 File langchain.vectorstore.base)
-      Function _similarity_search_with_relevance_scores is called from file langchain.vectorstore.faiss which in turn calls self.similarity_search_with_score function with the same query and the same threshold of 0.8
-     Function similarity_search_with_score is called which in turn calls self.similarity_search_with_score_by_vector with the same query and threshold of 0.8
-     self.similarity_search_with_score_by_vector is called with the threshold of 0.8 and the query. This function first fetches the docs till line 229 of langchain.vectorstore.faiss and then gets the score_threshold set which is still 0.8 in this case. It then matches and filters according to the threshold which is 0.8. 
- The score returned in the list docs with the similarities is not between 0 and 1 and thus comparing it to 0.8 will essentially not fetch any results. For example a score of 0.312 in the docs list similarity is actually 0.779 by using the function converter **1.0 - 0.312 / math.sqrt(2)** which is why we need to first convert the similarity score fetched by docs and then compare it to the incoming threshold.

body:
I noticed one thing while running the linter: 
`langchain/vectorstores/elasticsearch.py:783: error: Argument "source" to "search" of "Elasticsearch" has incompatible type "List[str]"; expected "Union[bool, Mapping[str, Any], None]"  [arg-type]` 

Looking at the client's search imlementation
```
def search(
      ...
      source: t.Optional[t.Union[bool, t.Mapping[str, t.Any]]] = None,
      ...
      source_includes: t.Optional[t.Union[str, t.Sequence[str]]] = None,
      ...
) -> ObjectApiResponse[t.Any]:
```
wouldn't it make sense to use the `source_includes` parameter to select fields to include in the response? Plus, I think it would also make mypy happy.

body:
Your approach over mine allows the following:
- Add new documents to QDrant collection that uses a single vector key `vector_name`.
- Query QDrant collection that uses any number of vector keys, using one pre-specified `vector_name`.

My approach in my second PR #5975 allows both scenarios but expands first to allow adding documents to collections that define multiple vector keys.

Now, my use case is already fulfilled with your work, because my use of `langchain` doesn't involve getting it to add new documents, but only for querying them. However, I would like you to understand the difference between both before you finalise your approach.
author: mahmoudajawad

body:
> Your approach over mine allows the following:
> 
>     * Add new documents to QDrant collection that uses a single vector key `vector_name`.
> 
>     * Query QDrant collection that uses any number of vector keys, using one pre-specified `vector_name`.
> 
> 
> My approach in my second PR #5975 allows both scenarios but expands first to allow adding documents to collections that define multiple vector keys.
> 
> Now, my use case is already fulfilled with your work, because my use of `langchain` doesn't involve getting it to add new documents, but only for querying them. However, I would like you to understand the difference between both before you finalise your approach.

We should not expose the vector configuration to the Langchain users. If anyone wants to use a custom configuration for their collection, it should be created directly with `QdrantClient` and then passed to `Qdrant` while being instantiated.

body:
Nice addition! As mentioned by @dev2049 we should updated the base VectorStore delete class [here](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/9ca1cf003cec61db147b2b473e71f82c974abe9c/langchain/vectorstores/base.py#L51) and above suggestion is reasonable to me. 
```
delete(self, ids: Optional[List[str]]=None, **kwargs: Any) 
```

A number of vectorstores have delete methods w/ ids passed, and this would of course be backwards compatible. But making IDs optional is more flexible for future additions, like the nice one you propose here.
author: rlancemartin

body:
I've refactored delete method in VectorStore and Pinecone classes to accept optional IDs and additional keyword arguments.
CC: @rlancemartin @dev2049 @eyurtsev
author: 0xcha05

body:
> Discussing w/ @nfcampos and @jacoblee93 who are adding this same functionality to js - there is possible confusion if a user passes both filter params and IDs. In this case, ID-wise delete is done first. Thoughts on having independent methods delete_by_id and delete_by_filter? Pinecone simply has delete as done here, so perhaps separate methods also has drawbacks. https://docs.pinecone.io/reference/delete_post

There could also be additional one-off options for other vector stores in the future - if we do separate methods we can always refactor to a single combined one if there's confusion.
author: jacoblee93

body:
the pinecone [doc](https://docs.pinecone.io/reference/delete_post) says that "If specified, the metadata filter here will be used to select the vectors to delete. This is mutually exclusive
with specifying IDs to delete in the ids param or using delete_all=True."
so we can just have the pinecone delete function with ids optional and kwargs, and have some validation in delete function of the pinecone class. what do you say? @rlancemartin
author: 0xcha05

body:
> the pinecone [doc](https://docs.pinecone.io/reference/delete_post) says that "If specified, the metadata filter here will be used to select the vectors to delete. This is mutually exclusive
> with specifying IDs to delete in the ids param or using delete_all=True."
> so we can just have the pinecone delete function with ids optional and kwargs, and have some validation in delete function of the pinecone class. what do you say?

I think it sounds fine for Pinecone and what devs are used to there, just not sure it's going to be so clean and clear as an interface extended to other vector stores since they might have different behavior/expected precedence for args like this and we're adding this to the base class.
author: jacoblee93

body:
Absolutely, let's clarify things a bit. The base delete function is only being tweaked to make ids optional and to allow for additional keyword arguments, like so:
`def delete(self, ids: Optional[List[str]] = None, **kwargs: Any) -> Optional[bool]:
`
Here's the [commit for reference](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/pull/6876/commits/964d72022d105772839c1a80af584279b118ad0b).

So, this change shouldn't disrupt any existing code that uses the delete method. The filter and delete_all parameters are specific to the Pinecone subclass and won't affect the base class or any other subclasses. Please correct me if I'm missing something!

body:
@hwchase17 Here are the sample code and errors with `langchain==0.0.209`. 

<details><summary>Sample Code and Errors</summary>

```python
from langchain.chat_models import ChatOpenAI
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from langchain.document_loaders import UnstructuredURLLoader
from langchain.chains.openai_functions import create_extraction_chain_pydantic

class LLMItem(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(description="The simple and concise title of the product")
    description: str = Field(description="The description of the product")

def main():

    llm = ChatOpenAI(temperature=0, model="gpt-3.5-turbo-16k")
    chain = create_extraction_chain_pydantic(pydantic_schema=LLMItem, llm=llm)

    loader = UnstructuredURLLoader(urls=["https://www.ebay.com/itm/115834603826"])
    data = loader.load()
    llm_item = chain.run(data)
    print(llm_item)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

```console
$ python test.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 21, in <module>
    main()

    llm_item = chain.run(data)

    return self(args[0], callbacks=callbacks, tags=tags)[_output_key]

  File "/Users/shotarokohama/.local/share/virtualenvs/.../python3.8/site-packages/langchain/chains/llm.py", line 93, in _call

  File "/Users/shotarokohama/.local/share/virtualenvs/.../python3.8/site-packages/langchain/chains/llm.py", line 217, in create_outputs

```

</details> 


And, we can confirm the function definition will include two nested `info` as shown below. The function definition led to ValidationError when parsing a response from OpenAI.

<details><summary>Print Function Definitions</summary>

```python
import json
from typing import List

from langchain.chains.openai_functions.utils import _resolve_schema_references
from langchain.chains.openai_functions.extraction import _get_extraction_function
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field


class Item(BaseModel):
    title: str = Field(description="The simple and concise title of the product")
    description: str = Field(description="The description of the product")

def main():
    class PydanticSchema(BaseModel):
        info: List[Item]

    openai_schema = PydanticSchema.schema()
    openai_schema = _resolve_schema_references(openai_schema, openai_schema["definitions"])
    function = _get_extraction_function(openai_schema)
    print(json.dumps(function))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

author: rlancemartin

body: @rlancemartin I updated docs, that was tricky. Looks like some docs related commands are outdated, and there is no clear info on how to add/build documentation.
author: skozlovf

body:
> @rlancemartin I updated docs, that was tricky. Looks like some docs related commands are outdated, and there is no clear info on how to add/build documentation.

Ah, I see you modified the .mdx files. What was tricky? Finding them? Realizing you don't need to build them? Also we should clarify that adding a Jupyter notebook in /docs/extras/modules is common practice (it gets converted to markdown automatically and added to our docs)? In any case, thanks for adding.
author: rlancemartin

body:
@rlancemartin 
> What was tricky?

As a LangChain internals noob, I would say that the main problem is the lack of info about the documentation structure  and how to build it. I got some understanding after digging through the sources, but it took time. This change is small, but I spent a lot of time figuring out how to update docs and validate changes. It would be nice to have a brief description of how this is all organized.

Here is the list of issues I met:

* I thought that docs are sphinx based but then found that it's assembled from multiple parts. `docs/.local_build.sh` helped here.
* Doc scripts look inconsistent and this adds some complicity to understanding the docs building flow. For example, doc related targets in `Makefile` are either outdated or incomplete: `docs_build: cd docs && poetry run make html`  but it should cd to `docs/api_reference` as I understand. Additional `docs/requirements.txt`, I thought all deps are in the `pyproject.toml`. Probably it's used by `vercel_build.sh` in a separate pipeline and it's not required for local build.
*  It took a while to realize that there are Jupyter notebooks and they are processed by `nbdoc`.
* It took time to understand that "main entry point" is `docusaurus`. It's not obvious how to prepare env for the non-frontend devs. For example, I was not able to build with the latest `yarn`, v1 works fine. Looks like it requires some config tweaking.
* Docs structure is still not very clear: docs root dir is `docs/docs_skeleton/`, some docs are located in the `./docs`, but others are outside the root dir in the `docs/extras` and `docs/modules`.

This could be a starting point if you plan to improve development docs. I can spawn a separate issue if you want to have some reference.

> I see you modified the .mdx files.

Yes. I didn't find source notebook (does it exist?) so I modified `.mdx` directly.
author: skozlovf

body: Yeah the docs have been overhauled in the past two weeks but the instructions haven't all been updated yet. Thank you for alerting us!
author: hinthornw

body:
> As a LangChain internals noob, I would say that the main problem is the lack of info about the documentation structure and how to build it. I got some understanding after digging through the sources, but it took time. This change is small, but I spent a lot of time figuring out how to update docs and validate changes. It would be nice to have a brief description of how this is all organized.
> 
> Here is the list of issues I met:
> 
> * I thought that docs are sphinx based but then found that it's assembled from multiple parts. `docs/.local_build.sh` helped here.
> * Doc scripts look inconsistent and this adds some complicity to understanding the docs building flow. For example, doc related targets in `Makefile` are either outdated or incomplete: `docs_build: cd docs && poetry run make html`  but it should cd to `docs/api_reference` as I understand. Additional `docs/requirements.txt`, I thought all deps are in the `pyproject.toml`. Probably it's used by `vercel_build.sh` in a separate pipeline and it's not required for local build.
> * It took a while to realize that there are Jupyter notebooks and they are processed by `nbdoc`.
> * It took time to understand that "main entry point" is `docusaurus`. It's not obvious how to prepare env for the non-frontend devs. For example, I was not able to build with the latest `yarn`, v1 works fine. Looks like it requires some config tweaking.
> * Docs structure is still not very clear: docs root dir is `docs/docs_skeleton/`, some docs are located in the `./docs`, but others are outside the root dir in the `docs/extras` and `docs/modules`.
> 
> This could be a starting point if you plan to improve development docs. I can spawn a separate issue if you want to have some reference.

Thanks for the detailed feedback. 

I shared w/ the a number of folk. 

We will work on making the instructions more clear. 

Usually folks will update notebooks [e.g., here for loaders](https://github.com/rlancemartin/langchain/tree/master/docs/extras/modules/data_connection/document_loaders/integrations), which get added to the docs automatically e.g., [here for loader docs](https://python.langchain.com/docs/modules/data_connection/document_loaders/).

body:
## PR Analysis

- 🎯 **Main theme:** Adding support for Amazon SageMaker Asynchronous Endpoints
- 🔍 **Description and title:** Yes
- 📌 **Type of PR:** Enhancement
- 🧪 **Relevant tests added:** No
- ✨ **Minimal and focused:** Yes
## PR Feedback

- 💡 **General PR suggestions:** The PR is well-structured and follows good coding practices. However, it lacks tests for the new functionality. It would be beneficial to add tests to ensure the new code works as expected and to prevent potential regressions in the future. Additionally, consider handling exceptions more gracefully, providing more informative error messages to the user.

- 🤖 **Code suggestions:**

- **suggestion 1:**
  - **relevant file:** sagemaker_endpoint.py
  - **suggestion content:** Consider moving the `wait_inference_file` function inside the `SagemakerAsyncEndpoint` class as a static or class method. This would improve the organization of the code and make it more object-oriented. [important]

- **suggestion 2:**
  - **relevant file:** sagemaker_endpoint.py
  - **suggestion content:** In the `wait_inference_file` function, instead of using a while True loop, consider using a for loop with a maximum number of retries to avoid potential infinite loops. [important]

- **suggestion 3:**
  - **relevant file:** sagemaker_endpoint.py
  - **suggestion content:** In the `__init__` method of `SagemakerAsyncEndpoint`, consider validating the input arguments to ensure they are in the correct format and within expected ranges. This can help catch errors early and provide clearer error messages to the user. [medium]

- **suggestion 4:**
  - **relevant file:** sagemaker_endpoint.py
  - **suggestion content:** In the `_call` method of `SagemakerAsyncEndpoint`, consider handling the exception when the endpoint is not running. Instead of raising a generic Exception, provide a more specific error message to the user. [medium]

- 🔒 **Security concerns:** No

Add a comment that says 'Please review' to ask for a new review after you update the PR.
Add a comment that says 'Please answer <QUESTION...>' to ask a question about this PR.

body: Requesting Langchain team's review and approval for PR  to add support for Sagemaker async endpoints. Let's leverage these cost-effective endpoints for enhanced performance! I am willing to support for any testing and validation. 🤝 Thank you!
author: bismillahkani

body: cc @3coins
author: baskaryan

body:
@dgallitelli 
Thanks for submitting this PR. 
It seems like the async endpoints are specifically geared towards large payloads (1GB) and long processing times. Is this something ideal for an LLM application? I am curious about what specific use cases will this integration help with?

How does this compare with SageMaker serverless inference which might also have the cost benefits?
author: 3coins

body:
> @dgallitelli Thanks for submitting this PR. It seems like the async endpoints are specifically geared towards large payloads (1GB) and long processing times. Is this something ideal for an LLM application? I am curious about what specific use cases will this integration help with?
> 
> How does this compare with SageMaker serverless inference which might also have the cost benefits?

If I may add my comment, the integration of AWS Sagemaker async endpoints holds promise for Langchain's LLM application. The support for GPU instances in async endpoints addresses the size and complexity of LLM models, unlike Sagemaker serverless inference which is limited to CPU instances. This distinction is significant for achieving optimal performance. Moreover, the cost-effectiveness achieved through scaling down to zero during idle periods enhances the feasibility of using AWS Sagemaker async.
author: bismillahkani

body: I think most of the vector stores "scores" by default are distances, though it sometimes depends on how you configure the underlying index. This is why the relevance scores method was created, as an api that enforces an interpretation of what score means
author: hinthornw

body:
I agree with you that relevance scores method was created to enforce an interpretation of what score means.

At the same time, I also think that since `VectorStore.similarity_search_with_relevance_scores()` treats score uniformly as similarity, the underlying implementation (Chroma in this case) should also return similarity instead of distance. 

https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/64039b9f112653d686bbcdd980fa90ad2f3eb9fc/langchain/vectorstores/base.py#L168-L174

> I think most of the vector stores "scores" by default are distances, though it sometimes depends on how you configure the underlying index. This is why the relevance scores method was created, as an api that enforces an interpretation of what score means
author: boxcounter

body:
@rlancemartin Not sure, I got it. download_documents() returns a list not an iterable.
I can do lazy_load from it. But converting the list to iterable and then back to the list?
author: leo-gan

body:
> @rlancemartin Not sure, I got it. download_documents() returns a list not an iterable.
> 
> I can do lazy_load from it. But converting the list to iterable and then back to the list?

Right. All of the new loaders have lazy_load method (useful in the case of loading a larger volume of docs since it returns a generator). But we keep load method for ease of use, which just calls lazy_load. You can see other examples such as [this](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/langchain/document_loaders/airtable.py). It's a minor change.
