Thanks . resolved. but there is another blocker. An integration test is failing. But I can see that in **_https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/langchain/vectorstores/chroma.py_** the _**similarity_search_by_vector_with_relevance_scores**_ is indeed available. what else could be causing it?

```
poetry run mypy .
tests/integration_tests/vectorstores/test_chroma.py:74: error: "Chroma" has no attribute "similarity_search_by_vector_with_relevance_scores"; maybe "_similarity_search_with_relevance_scores"?  [attr-defined]

make: *** [Makefile:34: lint] Error 1
Error: Process completed with exit code 2.
```

I also tested it locally , same error.

```
"""Test Chroma functionality."""
import pytest

from langchain.docstore.document import Document
from langchain.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain.embeddings import FakeEmbeddings

def test_chroma_with_metadatas_with_scores_using_vector() -> None:
    """Test end to end construction and scored search, using embedding vector."""
    texts = ["foo", "bar", "baz"]
    metadatas = [{"page": str(i)} for i in range(len(texts))]
    embeddings = FakeEmbeddings(size=5)

    docsearch = Chroma.from_texts(
        collection_name="test_collection",
        texts=texts,
        embedding=embeddings,
        metadatas=metadatas,
    )
    embedded_query = embeddings.embed_query("foo")
    output = docsearch.similarity_search_by_vector_with_relevance_scores(
        embedding=embedded_query, k=1
    )
    assert output == [(Document(page_content="foo", metadata={"page": "0"}), 0.0)]

test_chroma_with_metadatas_with_scores_using_vector()

```

author: vercel

body: This is a good general theme in retrieval: how to enforce diversity among the retrieved docs? MMR should be effective for a single retriever, but it would be interesting to test this vs [MMR](https://medium.com/tech-that-works/maximal-marginal-relevance-to-rerank-results-in-unsupervised-keyphrase-extraction-22d95015c7c5). In the case of multiple retrievers, like you show, dupes will be unavoidable even if each is using MMR. So, this type of clustering / de-dupe as a post-processing stage makes sense. What other ideas along these lines did you consider?
author: rlancemartin

body:
> 

Hi Lance! The de-dupe is an interesting effect I was using the good old Redundant filter too. But, what had the most value for me was to control the equilibrium of redundancy / duplication.
With clustering and a dynamic "n" sample per cluster you can "choose" diff "portions". Dealing with merger_retriever or dealing with a giant collection that you must check completely like during a summarization, to be able to take samples that are diverse and representative of the whole corpus just in the right amount. The de-dupe along it's just one side of the coin.
author: GMartin-dev

body:
This is super cool! Just here to follow along.

Awesome work @GMartin-dev and @rlancemartin
author: gkamradt

body:
> The de-dupe is an interesting effect I was using the good old Redundant filter too

Right, when I say de-dupe I also mean "semantically" de-dupe (in addition to de-dupe identical chunks): like you are saying, we will compress the retrieved docs to enforce diversity among the results. Also, looks like @gkamradt used it initially in the context of summarization. 

So, overall, neat idea for compression that it seems we could use in at least two places: (1) pre-processing of chunks (e.g., from a large doc, like a book) that are passed to LLM for summarization (like @gkamradt used it for). (2) post-processing of retrieved docs (esp using merge retriever), which can enforce diversity (like MMR does) / compress semantically similar results. 

Any other uses you guys had in mind?
author: rlancemartin

np! looks like an issue w/ `poetry.lock`?

```
Error: poetry.lock is not consistent with pyproject.toml. Run `poetry lock [--no-update]` to fix it.
```

try this:
```
git checkout master poetry.lock
poetry lock --no-update
```

gets the latest `poetry.lock` and updates it w/ your `pyproject.toml`.

body: thanks for opening this @wewebber-merlin! Do we know if root cause of this is on OpenAI side or LangChain side?
author: baskaryan

body:
Hi @baskaryan !  

The root cause here is definitely the OpenAI side: I assume for some reason their embeddings service fails and produces nonsense output, but they fail to check for it and instead return it to the user as if it succeeded.  

That said, this failure is relatively frequent---we're processing large volumes (100s of 1000s) of largish documents (emails and attachments), and seeing the failure perhaps twice per thousand documents---and the current langchain API provides no reasonable way of dealing with this that is not more complicated than bypassing langchain and making the calls directly to OpenAI.

Nevertheless, I'm happy to kick this upstairs (i.e. pursue with OpenAI) if you prefer not to check for and handle this behavior in langchain.

I also note a lint failure due to lack of type annotations; I will correct if you do wish to proceed with this patch.

author: vercel

body:
> this is cool, but not sure we want to introduce a new dependency for this

@hwchase17 Yeah, I hear you. It is a fairly light-weight and stable dependency, though.

Pros:
- Makes the code easier to read
- Reduces the risk of defects surrounding timer logic
- Reduces duplicate timout logic
- Sets a precedent. As LangChain continues to grow, there's likely to be more timers. Starting to use this now could keep future timer-related code simpler and help reduce defects.
- EggTimer handles edge cases most developers may not think of. For example, what happens to the code in this PR if the system clock is changed during the timeout window? What is the impact of that behavior? That issue can be simply resolved without EggTimer, but with EggTimer you don't need to worry about it.

Cons:
- New dependency

It's up to you whether or not the benefits are worth the cost. Writing timeouts is not rocket science; duplicating the timeout logic throughout the code is unlikely to result in a catastrophic failure down the road.

body:
@leo-gan Looks like there are some failures because `AgentIterator` calls a protected method on `AgentExecutor`, which breaks encapsulation.

https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/blob/5272e42b0de2a056271ebea8245cba1e29863621/libs/langchain/langchain/agents/agent_iterator.py#L475-L476

Options are:
1. Don't do that! Handle this logic within `AgentIterator` itself.
2. Make `AgentExecutor._should_continue()` public
3. Something else?

Of course, this is all dependent upon the project's willingness to accept EggTimer as a dependency.

author: rlancemartin

body:
`max_tokens` is the same as `n_predict`. According to the docs `n_predict` exists for backwards compatibility, so you can safely replace it with `max_tokens`.
However, `n_ctx` refers to the size of the models' window - maximum number of tokens in conversation that a model can "remember". Models have an upper bound for this param, if it goes beyond it, it can lead to a strange output, but it's fine to have a smaller bound.
idk if they will support this param. You can remove it I guess.
Edit: there's an issue for that: https://github.com/nomic-ai/gpt4all/issues/668
author: Chae4ek

body:
> nomic-ai/gpt4all#668

Ya, I've used `n_ctx` with other Llama models. I'm a bit confused though: AFIACT, GPT4All is broken on master b/c we are passing `n_ctx` to the `generate` method of the GPT4All client. Is this reproduced by others? Did their client change? I assume this used to be a valid parameter given that we have it.  If others were seeing this bug using GPT4All, I assume there would have been more urgency to fix ASAP (though I'm not sure when it was introduced, maybe it was with a very recent update and I just happened to get unlucky?). Today was the first time I tried using GPT4All and I noticed that it seems to be non-functional due to this bug :-P ... I'm not sure I am missing something, but I am able to get the model working if I remove the `n_ctx` parameter.
author: rlancemartin

body:
Here's the breaking change: https://github.com/nomic-ai/gpt4all/commit/46a0762bd5a7e605e9bd63e4f435b482eff026f6#diff-8408b8ad7c4660cb748f61513e5aa4e38cc3cee45f658d28eb16a72c2c47c391L190
not so long ago.

I was looking through their code for fun and it was never implemented. In fact, `n_ctx` is set from the model file, i.e. the `n_ctx` param doesn't affect anything. I hope they will do it though
author: Chae4ek

Nice find, thanks for clarifying!
author: rlancemartin

body:
> Here's the breaking change: [nomic-ai/gpt4all@46a0762#diff-8408b8ad7c4660cb748f61513e5aa4e38cc3cee45f658d28eb16a72c2c47c391L190](https://github.com/nomic-ai/gpt4all/commit/46a0762bd5a7e605e9bd63e4f435b482eff026f6#diff-8408b8ad7c4660cb748f61513e5aa4e38cc3cee45f658d28eb16a72c2c47c391L190) not so long ago.
> 
> I was looking through their code for fun and it was never implemented. In fact, `n_ctx` is set from the model file, i.e. the `n_ctx` param doesn't affect anything. I hope they will do it though

One thing I should be wary of: this PR will cause a breakage for users that (1) update LangChain but (2) do not update the Nomic / GPT4All client. I can add some error handling to make it backwards compatible.
author: rlancemartin

body: Would it make sense to add a PR to `gpt4all` to add a `langchain` test to help mitigate future breaking changes?
author: transitive-bullshit

body:
This all looks kosher above. Thanks for the quick fix.

 n_ctx is read only parameter that should have never been exposed in gpt4all. The c backend manages it, not the user.

Setting it previously had no effect.

The major version bump of gpt4all now exposes only read/write parameters in generate and should in general be faster.

Please take a look at the chat session mode which makes chatbot session inference much faster by preserving KV caches. Maybe this has a place in langchain.

body: Hey @baskaryan can I get some feedback on this pr, perhaps I could write some documentation for the other functions of the Pinecone integration to make this pr worth pushing? When I picked up Langchain the Pinecone docs didn't give much info, it would save future Pinecone users a lot of time! Thanks :)
author: conboy

body:
> this looks great to me - i think also making sure the reference page has good documentation (ie in the class docstrings) is high value: https://api.python.langchain.com/en/latest/vectorstores/langchain.vectorstores.pinecone.Pinecone.html#langchain.vectorstores.pinecone.Pinecone

@hwchase17 Great, thanks for the input, I'll make another issue for more functions/examples to be put on the Pinecone docs page. Speaking from experience, I came to the Pinecone docs page (as a Langchain noob) from a hackathon and it wasn't very helpful to create CRUD-like logic for a REST API (which I assume is a big use case for Pinecone). I had to look through Pinecone docs and Langchain's source code to get the logic down. So I hope to make the docs more indicative for users by adding more examples. :)

Another suggestion I have is to have some references in the docs for making Pinecone indexes/namespaces so Langchain users don't have to cross-reference Pinecone and Langchain docs.

In terms of Langchain's Pinecone API reference I'll take a look at that and try to improve that too. 👍

body:
@felixocker thank you for this PR! I would like to kindly ask if you can help me with a related issue. I keep receiving warning messages from rdflib when loading an RDF file even if URIs are valid.
```
<http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/title does not look like a valid URI, trying to serialize this will break.
<http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/title> ( does not look like a valid URI, trying to serialize this will break.
<http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/title> (title does not look like a valid URI, trying to serialize this will break.
<http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/title> (title,  does not look like a valid URI, trying to serialize this will break.
<http://xmlns.com/foaf/0.1/title> (title, ) does not look like a valid URI, trying to serialize this will break.
```
I did some tests locally and it seems to me that the problem may be related to the implementation of the `_res_to_str` function. I modified this as follows and it seems to work fine (`return f"<{res[var]}> ({self._get_local_name(res[var])}, {str(res['com'])})"`).
author: marioscrock

body: @marioscrock thanks! yeah, seems like this is due to the concatenation of strings and the URI in the `_res_to_str` function. I just created PR #8025 with a fix
author: felixocker

body: @felixocker thank you for the quick feedback and fix!

author: rlancemartin

body:
> tests please!

`parse_and_check_json_markdown` is actually used in several places that and output always expects a `dict`:

```
docs/api_reference/api_reference.rst:    output_parsers.json.parse_and_check_json_markdown
langchain/chains/query_constructor/base.py:from langchain.output_parsers.json import parse_and_check_json_markdown
langchain/chains/query_constructor/base.py:            parsed = parse_and_check_json_markdown(text, expected_keys)
langchain/chains/router/llm_router.py:from langchain.output_parsers.json import parse_and_check_json_markdown
langchain/chains/router/llm_router.py:            parsed = parse_and_check_json_markdown(text, expected_keys)
```

to limit the scope / risk, i break out a new function `parse_and_check_multiple_json_markdown` that will parse multiple JSON objects from the string and use it selectively w/ `StructuredOutputParser` where there is no risk of breakage (parse can return either a dict or a list of dicts, and has been tested). 

of course, if we want to migrate all instances of `parse_and_check_json_markdown` to be compatible w/ either a dict (single object) or multiple objects, we can take that up in a follow-up PR; i don't have full context on all the various usages of `parse_and_check_json_markdown`, so am a bit wary making a more sweeping change here.
author: rlancemartin

body:
> sorry what does this do/allow?

When we use LlamaCpp and we need embeddings, earlier we load model in LlamaCpp in memory, then we create LlamaCppEmbeddings and load the same model again. After this modification we can create LlamaCppEmbeddings with existing LlamaCpp model and use as always. Reduce needed memory, reduce time for loading.

body:
i think we probably want to find a way to do this with callbacks. what is the end state youre hoping to achieve?

cc @agola11 @nfcampos @vowelparrot
author: hwchase17

body:
@hwchase17 The problem I'm basically having, and maybe it's a documentation problem, is that I need to line up every single call to OpenAI with a corresponding template and parameters to that template, and the parameters passed to the callbacks don't seem to line up with these calls. This is mostly based on my understanding than an "LLMChain" can call an LLM more than once per call to the chain.

* `on_chain_start`: I get a dictionary in the `inputs` but I have to dig around in `serialized` in order to find the template (it is more or less at `serialized["kwargs"]["prompt"]["kwargs"]` which feels very fragile. In addition I'm making some assumptions here:
  * when there is a cache, it seems like `on_chain_start` might get called when there is a cache hit, and openai is never called
  * From the docs it seems like there might be more than one call to the llm for a single chain run? i.e. a chain might make 2 calls to the LLM, and potentially the inputs could be different?
* `on_llm_start`: I found this wasn't being called at all because I'm using Model I/O (specifically `langchain.chat_models. ChatOpenAI`) - but I was able to pass in the same callbacks object instance that I was passing to the chain in order to get this passed in (see below)
  * the passed in parameters are mostly the raw parameters to OpenAI, namely the prompt, temperature, etc, all already resolved and no template + args available.

My pseudocode:
```python
from langchain.chat_models import ChatOpenAI

class MyCallbacks(BaseCallbackHandler):
    def on_chain_start(..):
         # called with template parameters
    def on_llm_start(...):
         # called with raw llm parameters

callbacks = MyCallbacks()
llm = ChatOpenAI(..., callbacks=[callbacks])
chain = LLMChain(llm=llm,...,callbacks=[callbacks])
```

etc..
author: alecf

body:
I think I've figured out a better way to do this with callbacks by retaining state in the callbacks object

Thanks for the help

body:
## PR Analysis

- 🎯 **Main theme:** Fixing the Pydantic parser to handle illegal quotations in JSON strings
- 🔍 **Description and title:** Yes
- 📌 **Type of PR:** Bug fix
- 🧪 **Relevant tests added:** Yes
- ✨ **Minimal and focused:** Yes, the PR is focused on fixing a specific issue with the Pydantic parser and all changes are relevant to this issue.
- 🔒 **Security concerns:** No, the PR does not introduce any obvious security concerns.
## PR Feedback

- 💡 **General PR suggestions:** The PR is well-structured and the changes are relevant to the issue at hand. The new function `_remove_illegal_quatations` is a good approach to handle the issue. However, the function could be improved by handling edge cases and making the code more readable.

- 🤖 **Code suggestions:**


  - **relevant file:** langchain/output_parsers/pydantic.py
   **suggestion content:** Consider adding a docstring to the `_remove_illegal_quatations` function to explain its purpose and how it works. [medium]


  - **relevant file:** langchain/output_parsers/pydantic.py
   **suggestion content:** The `_remove_illegal_quatations` function assumes that there are always at least four quotation marks in a line. This might not always be the case. Consider adding a check to handle lines with less than four quotation marks. [important]


  - **relevant file:** langchain/output_parsers/pydantic.py
   **suggestion content:** The `_remove_illegal_quatations` function could be made more readable by using named constants for the indices instead of numbers. For example, `FIRST_QUOTE_INDEX = 0`, `SECOND_QUOTE_INDEX = 1`, etc. [medium]


  - **relevant file:** tests/unit_tests/output_parsers/test_pydantic_parser.py
   **suggestion content:** Consider adding more test cases to cover different scenarios and edge cases. For example, a test case where a line has less than four quotation marks. [important]


### How to use
> Tag me in a comment '@CodiumAI-Agent' to ask for a new review after you update the PR.
> You can also tag me and ask any question, for example '@CodiumAI-Agent is the PR ready for merge?'

author: vercel

body:
> This returns citations:
> 
> ```
> from langchain.chains import RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain
> qa_chain = RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain.from_chain_type(llm,retriever=vectorstore.as_retriever())
> result = qa_chain({"question": question})
> ```
> 
> This returns source documents:
> 
> ```
> from langchain.chains import RetrievalQA
> qa_chain = RetrievalQA.from_chain_type(llm,retriever=vectorstore.as_retriever(),
>                                        return_source_documents=True)
> result = qa_chain({"query": question})
> print(len(result['source_documents']))
> result['source_documents'][0]
> ```
> 
> Do those cover the use-case you need here?

No. The `RetrievalQA` or `RetrievalQAWithSourceChain` return only the url of **all** documents used.
But, 
- some documents have no URL
- not all documents are used
- Others metadata can be important to manipulate the response.

With my proposition (next version soon), return only the documents used to answer, and, if it's possible, the *verbatim* used to answer.
And, it's possible to manipulate all the information about these documents (Google file id, records id from DB, etc.)
author: pprados

body:
Dear @rlancemartin,

I have made several changes to the code for this new release.

Before explaining the changes, let me provide some context. With `RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain` or `RetrievalQA`, it was not possible to identify only the documents used, as the parameter `return_source_documents=True` would return all documents, even those not used for the response. Additionally, with `RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain`, only the `metadata["source"]` was returned, making it difficult to calculate the link between the URL and the corresponding document, especially when multiple documents shared the same URL. And, it's possible ONLY if the metadata has `source`.

That's why I'm proposing this new version (which fixes a bug in `RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain` when the recursive process is necessary).

New release:
- To optimize token usage, I have stopped using UUIDs (UUIDs consume tokens).
- Instead, I now use an index from the list of documents, which is shorter.
- The code is now compatible with asynchronous operations.
- I have also updated the documentation in `docs/extras/use_cases/question_answering/index.mdx`.
- When using `chain_type='stuff'`, all documents are merged into the same prompt, and only the documents used are returned.
- For `chain_type='map_reduce'`, the verbatim is extracted from the document to answer and added to the metadata. This allows searching for the portion of text used for the response. If the list of documents is large, the recursive approach is used, and the verbatim may not be extracted.
- `chain_type='refine'` cannot extract the verbatim, but it only returns the documents used.
- Lastly, `chain_type='map_rerank'` returns only the best document.
- I fixed a bug in `llm.py` related to empty document lists.
- Now, it's possible to create a link, with the source and the [text fragment](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/Text_fragments) to highlight the portion of page (url with `#:~:text=...`).

I hope you find these changes beneficial.

Later, if you accept my code, it will be possible to revise `RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain` to inherit `RetrievalQAWithReferencesChain`.
author: pprados

body: Note that another difference with qa_with_source is the consumption of tokens. When you add a URL for each document or aggregate a list of URLs, especially if you have many documents, it can consume a significant number of tokens. In my proposition, only an integer is used to identify the corresponding document. This approach can be more efficient when dealing with a big list of documents, allowing for better performance.
author: pprados

author: pprados

body:
> Hello @rlancemartin, I have a "1 change requested", but i can not change something. Is the process blocked?

@pprados thanks for the detailed explanation. 

Also adding @baskaryan and @eyurtsev who may have a point of view. 

AFAICT, the problem you want to solve: 
* return the exact source documents used for answer generation in RAG.

Limitations today:
* `return_source_documents=True` returns **all** source docs retrieved 
* `RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain` returns citations (IIUC also for **all** source docs retrieved?)

Question 1:
* What is the precise problem with `RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain`? 
* Can we put up a PR that addresses it narrowly? 
* This should resolve your use case, IIUC.

Approach:
* You use `map_reduce` or `refine` chains w/ `intermediate_steps=True`
* `"verbatim"` of the docs is retrieved from the `answers["intermediate_steps"]`
* Thus, the question answer for each document is used 

Question 2:
* This seems to be wrapping MR or Refine chains `intermediate_steps`, which is the question answer per doc.
* That is logged to `verbatim`.
* But additional work is needed to compare each to the final answer to see which docs are most related.

In short, the premise is good: higher quality citations for RAG generations. The problem w/ `RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain` is not fully obvious to me, but I also have not worked with it extensively. A crisp overview of that problem and PR to address it narrowly seems reasonable. It seems you found a bug there? And IIUC, it will return citations of all doc?

Use of MR or Refine `intermediate_steps` is also not entirely obvious to me as a solution to the problem: as you show in the notebook, you will get an answer per document for the question (logged to `'verbatim'`) but it's still not fully obvious how each answer maps to the final answer without some additional work (e.g., semantic similarity, etc) that will add further overhead.
author: rlancemartin

body:
Question 1:

What is the precise problem with RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain?
- The challenge lies in returning the precise source documents employed for generating answers in RAG.
- RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain solely provides URLs (requiring documents to possess URLs) without additional information.
- Its structure is defined by the implementation, manipulating a list of URLs rather than a list of referenced documents.
- My implementation establishes a direct correlation between references and associated documents.

Can we put up a PR that addresses it narrowly? 
- Yes, although the PR can only be submitted after acceptance.
- A potential solution involves reconfiguring RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain as a derivative of my code. This simplified approach involves using RetrievalQAWithReference to acquire the list of original documents and extracting solely the URLs. This implementation proves advantageous as it reduces token consumption (replacing each URL in the list with a numerical value).

Approach:

You use map_reduce or refine chains w/ intermediate_steps=True
- Indeed, this choice is driven by the capability of map-reduce mode to extract the relevant document portions. However, accessing this information requires setting `intermediate_steps=True`. My implementation repurposes the code from RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain. The mapping process employs a Language Model (LLM) to extract a focused section from each document, along with the verbatim answer. This verbatim answer, at times "nothing" and at others more substantive, is then employed in the reduction phase to formulate the final answer. However, it is unnecessary to incorporate the verbatim into the response body, as its retrieval is possible with intermediate_steps=True.

"verbatim" of the docs is retrieved from the answers["intermediate_steps"]
- Yes, this corresponds to the same approach employed in RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain.

Question 2:

This seems to be wrapping MR or Refine chains intermediate_steps, which is the question answer per doc.
That is logged to verbatim.
But additional work is needed to compare each to the final answer to see which docs are most related.
- Not exactly. The reduction phase selects the corresponding document (the exact document from which the verbatim was extracted - usually, a simple text search can locate this verbatim in the original document). It is the role of the Language Model to answer the question of which document corresponds to the question-answer pair per document. This is analogous to RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain, albeit using an ID instead of a URL.

It seems you found a bug there?
- Yes, the current implementation of RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain struggles to handle a list of URLs for each document. Yet, when utilizing map-reduce, an initial reduction step can be applied. This step employs multiple documents and yields a partial response containing a list of documents. This response then becomes a new document for further analysis, and another reduce phase. Consequently, with a substantial list of documents, using a partial reduction can result in the loss of the complete URL list (the templace accept only one url by document). However, the primary issue here pertains to the number of tokens consumed in this scenario (an URL like from GoogleDoc, are not LLM friendly).

I believe hallucinations pose a significant issue. Enabling straightforward referencing of what led to the answer's derivation is imperative. Relying solely on a list of documents, some utilized and some not, is an inadequate approach.
author: pprados

There are some interesting ideas here that it would be great to allow folks to experiment further with them.

author: pprados
pprados

body:
@baskaryan, @rlancemartin,
(I changed the description for this new version)
    
# Question answering with references and verbatim over documents.

We believe that **hallucinations** pose a major problem in the adoption of LLMs (Language Model Models). 
It is imperative to provide a simple and quick solution that allows the user to verify the coherence of the answers to the questions they are asked.

This chain extracts the information from the documents that was used to answer the
question. The output `source_documents` contains only the documents that were used,
and for each one, only the text fragments that were used to answer are included.
If possible, the list of text fragments that justify the answer is added to
`metadata['verbatims']` for each document.
Then it is possible to find the page of a PDF, or the chapter of a markdown.

A sample result of usage with Wikipedia, may be:
```
For the question "what can you say about ukraine?",
to answer "Ukraine has an illegal and unprovoked invasion inside its territory,
and its citizens show courage and are fighting against it. It is believed that
the capital city of Kyiv, which is home to 2.8 million people, is a target.",
the LLM use:
Source https://www.defense.gov/News/Transcripts/...
-  "when it comes to conducting their illegal and unprovoked invasion inside
Ukraine."
Source https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/...
- "to the fearless and skilled Ukrainian fighters who are standing in the
breach"
- "You got to admit, you have -- must be amazed at the courage of this country"
Source https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/...
-  "believe that they will target Ukraine's capital, Kyiv, a city of 2.8 million
innocent people."
Source https://www.whitehouse.gov/briefing-room/...
-  "believe that they will target Ukraine's capital, Kyiv, a city of 2.8 million
innocent people."
```

author: leo-gan

body:
Hello @leo-gan

Thank you for considering my proposal. 

For the moment, it is on the experimental branch, but I am hopeful of a deeper integration into langchain. Then, it will be possible to add 2 methods to `VectorStoreIndexWrapper`: `query_with_references()` and `query_with_references_and_verbatims()`

Some remarks to understand the code.
- It is a fork of `qa_with_sources`. I resynchronized my code with the latest version.
- It is not very clear whether it is possible/advisable to use parsers from templates. The current implementations of `ReduceDocumentsChain` and `MapReduceDocumentsChain` do not use prompt parsers, and that's a shame.  
- `MapRerankDocumentsChain` use `apply_and_parse()` but it's deprecated. I would adjust the code in sync with `qa_with_sources`.
- An evolution of these 3 classes will be welcome and would simplify my code. I can do it, but i would be sure it's what you want: use the associated parser for eachs prompts.

For `qa_with_reference`, I propose 2 implementations, one based on Pydantic objects, the other without in order to save token consumption. This is the version that is activated.

For `qa_with_reference_and_verbatims`, I use a Pydantic objects and the corresponding parser.

Integration tests require `openai` and `wikipedia` (`pip install openai tiktoken wikipedia`). You can see the different results with `qa_with_sources`, `qa_with_references` and `qa_with_sources_and_verbatims`. 

Sometime, `qa_with_sources` in mode  `map_rerank` crash. Because the regex pattern is '(.*?)**\\n**Score: (\\d*)' but sometime, the LLM returns the score, without the `\n'. It may be fixed, but I don't want to create confusion.

After validation, it will be possible to review `qa_with_sources` for a compatible implementation, but more economical in tokens, without bugs in the case of a large list of documents (bug because it's impossible to manage a long list of URL, and recursive map-reduce is not prepared to use multiple URLs for the same document).
In addition, the new version will only be able to find the list of documents used for the response (and not the list of documents returned by the vectorstore. It is not the same thing - some documents are not really used).

Thanks @pprados - I took a quick look at your codes - looks great.

~~However, I did notice your implementation on `QAWithReferencesAndVerbatimsChain` deviates quite a bit from the rest of LangChain's `qa_chain` or `ConversationalRetrievalChain`. For example, there's no argument/concept of `retriever` anymore.~~

Never mind, I see you also have `RetrievalQAWithReferencesAndVerbatimsChain` implemented.
author: timxieICN

body: @pprados - also, what's the technical reason why verbatim only works for `chain_type='map_reduce'`?
author: timxieICN

body:
@pprados  - I tried to run your code by refactoring it into our existing workflow, as follows:

```
query_executor = RetrievalQAWithReferencesAndVerbatimsChain.from_llm(
                llm=AzureChatOpenAI(temperature=0),
                retriever=OpenSearchVectorStore,
                memory=...,
                chain_type="map_reduce"
)
```

I noticed:
1. The code is quite slow. For QA on a 8-page long pdf, the query can easily take up to 30 sec.
2. The response is not always optimal. The response is not as accurate as what the `ConversationalRetrievalChain` provides.

Is this what you've seen as well?
author: timxieICN

body:
btw, @pprados - are you aware of this https://api.python.langchain.com/en/latest/_modules/langchain/chains/openai_functions/citation_fuzzy_match.html?

I tested it a bit and it seems more generalized than your approach.
author: timxieICN

body:
@timxieICN 

> also, what's the technical reason why verbatim only works for chain_type='map_reduce'?

No. It's works with all chain_type. It's an all comment. I will fix it.

I will try the other implementations.

body:
**Bito AI analyzed this PR as follows.**

The change added an optional  `api_key`  parameter to the  `OpenAIWhisperParser`  class. This allows passing an OpenAI API key to the parser. If provided, the API key is set using  `openai.api_key = self.api_key`  before making requests to the OpenAI API.
 This change fixes the parser issue requiring an OpenAI API key to be set as an environment variable. Accepting the API key as a parameter makes the parser more flexible, and the API key does not need to be set as an environment variable. 
 
A potential risk is that by accepting the API key as a parameter, it needs to be passed by the user and could potentially be exposed. To mitigate this, the calling code should handle the API key securely.

An improvement could be accepting the API key from an environment variable as a fallback. This would provide more flexibility, allowing the API key to be passed as a parameter or set as an environment variable. The parameter value would take precedence over the environment variable.

This output is generated automatically by analyzing the diff patch on a model grounded to the LangChain codebase. This is meant to be helpful for the community, and the solution may not be 100% accurate.

body:
@hwchase17 sorry i messed up the issue #7020 .

> love this idea, few questions on implementation
> 
> biggest one: @nfcampos wdyt about changing this to just use sequential chain? that would let it log intermediate steps properly, and we can make it easy to go from arbitrary chain to tool (if we don't already).

i checked sequential chain and some other chain implementation and yes really sequential chain seems to working on quite a similar task.

the initial idea to introduce pipeline tool (or perhaps shall i change it to sequential tool ), is that we want leverage existing restful services in and outside our team. with request tools that 
can be easily done. 

and chain seems to be a little bit heavy as most of the examples and implemented chain classess seem to be relevant to LLM tasks. the pipeline tool could be lightweight just to lookup sth and transform the output into a more readable document to agents.

for the intermediate steps, it seems the mechanism is not implemented in the base class. but within current pipeline tool implementation, as long as the intemediate steps are desired, they could be kept in the output as well.

body:
## PR Analysis

- 🎯 **Main theme:** Adding wrapper functions for OpenAI function in LLMChain and an additional class for the output parser
- 🔍 **Description and title:** Yes
- 📌 **Type of PR:** Enhancement
- 🧪 **Relevant tests added:** No
- ✨ **Minimal and focused:** Yes, the PR is focused on adding new functionalities to the LLMChain and does not include unrelated changes.
- 🔒 **Security concerns:** No, the PR does not introduce any obvious security concerns.
## PR Feedback

- 💡 **General PR suggestions:** The PR is generally well-structured and follows good coding practices. However, it lacks tests for the new functionalities added. It would be beneficial to add tests to ensure the new functions and class behave as expected.

- 🤖 **Code suggestions:**


  - **relevant file:** langchain/chains/openai_functions/converter.py
   **suggestion content:** Consider adding type hints for the 'pydantic_schema' parameter in the 'from_pydantic_create_openai_function_dict' function. This will improve code readability and help prevent potential type-related bugs. [important]


  - **relevant file:** langchain/chains/openai_functions/converter.py
   **suggestion content:** The 'from_json_create_openai_function_dict' function currently assumes that the input is a valid JSON string. It would be a good idea to add error handling for the case where 'json.loads' fails due to an invalid input. [important]


  - **relevant file:** langchain/output_parsers/openai_functions.py
   **suggestion content:** In the 'PydanticOutputFunctionsParserToString' class, the 'parse_result' method converts the result to a string. Consider handling the case where 'pydantic_args.dict()' fails or returns a non-string convertible value. [medium]


  - **relevant file:** langchain/chains/openai_functions/converter.py
   **suggestion content:** The 'from_dict_create_openai_function_dict' function modifies the input dictionary by adding a 'type' key. This can lead to unexpected side effects if the caller does not expect the input to be modified. Consider creating a copy of the input dictionary before modifying it. [important]

> You can also tag me and ask any question, for example '@CodiumAI-Agent is the PR ready for merge?'

It's important to add tests to ensure the new functions work as expected and to prevent future regressions. Also, consider adding more detailed docstrings for the new functions, explaining what each argument is and what the function returns.

- 🤖 **Code suggestions:**


  - **relevant file:** langchain/chains/openai_functions/converter.py
   **suggestion content:** In the `from_json_create_openai_function_dict` function, you're catching all exceptions when trying to parse the JSON. It would be better to catch specific exceptions (like `json.JSONDecodeError`) to avoid hiding potential bugs. [important]


  - **relevant file:** langchain/chains/openai_functions/converter.py
   **suggestion content:** In the `from_dict_create_openai_function_dict` function, use `isinstance(dict_schema, dict)` instead of `type(dict_schema) != dict` for type checking. This is more Pythonic and allows for subclassing. [medium]


  - **relevant file:** langchain/output_parsers/openai_functions.py
   **suggestion content:** In the `PydanticOutputFunctionsParserToString` class, you're converting the parsed result to a string. This could lead to loss of information if the parsed result is a complex object. Consider returning the raw object and let the caller decide how to handle it. [medium]


  - **relevant file:** langchain/chains/openai_functions/converter.py
   **suggestion content:** The three functions `from_pydantic_create_openai_function_dict`, `from_json_create_openai_function_dict`, and `from_dict_create_openai_function_dict` have a lot of common code. Consider refactoring them to reduce code duplication. [medium]

author: baskaryan

body:
hey @baskaryan! looks good. One thing that probably needs to change is the memory system. I don't know if you guys took a look into this but in `ChatOpenAI`, the `AIMessage` is appended as a `string`, so the output parser [here](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/langchain/chains/openai_functions/base.py#L163C54-L163C54) would actually fail since the output is a pydantic class.

I added [this](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/e78ce0d2ecdc0b1a3a6d514da4bdc22c54b9e64d/langchain/output_parsers/openai_functions.py#L52C1-L53C1) temporary get around to solve this but let me know if you have any other ideas about this!
author: enjuichang

body:
> hey @baskaryan! looks good. One thing that probably needs to change is the memory system. I don't know if you guys took a look into this but in `ChatOpenAI`, the `AIMessage` is appended as a `string`, so the output parser [here](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/master/langchain/chains/openai_functions/base.py#L163C54-L163C54) would actually fail since the output is a pydantic class.
> 
> I added [this](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/blob/e78ce0d2ecdc0b1a3a6d514da4bdc22c54b9e64d/langchain/output_parsers/openai_functions.py#L52C1-L53C1) temporary get around to solve this but let me know if you have any other ideas about this!

hm yea interesting point. my first thought is i'd probably create a custom memory that would convert the original output to str before saving it. that way you can still use the structured output in downstream logic

something like 
```python
class StructuredChatMemory(BaseChatMemory):

    def _get_input_output(
      self, inputs: Dict[str, Any], outputs: Dict[str, str]
    ) -> Tuple[str, str]        
      input, output = super()._get_input_output(inputs, outputs)
      if isinstance(output, BaseModel):
        output = output.json(indent=2)
      return input, output
```


what do you think?

## PR Analysis

- 🎯 **Main theme:** Fixing a bug where OpenAIFunctionsAgent often returns python as a tool in planning, when argument parsing fails
- 🔍 **Description and title:** Yes
- 📌 **Type of PR:** Bug fix
- 🧪 **Relevant tests added:** No
- ✨ **Minimal and focused:** Yes, the PR is focused on fixing a specific bug in the OpenAIFunctionsAgent's argument parsing
- 🔒 **Security concerns:** No, the changes made in this PR do not introduce any obvious security concerns
## PR Feedback

- 💡 **General PR suggestions:** The PR is well-structured and solves the problem at hand. However, it would be beneficial to add tests to ensure the bug is fixed and prevent future regressions. Also, the use of a hack to solve the problem might not be the best approach in the long term. It would be better to understand why the arguments become a string and fix the root cause.

- 🤖 **Code suggestions:**


  - **relevant file:** base.py
   **suggestion content:** Instead of using a hack to solve the problem, investigate why the arguments become a string for the 'python' tool. This might be a symptom of a deeper issue that needs to be addressed. [important]


  - **relevant file:** base.py
   **suggestion content:** Add error handling for the case where the function_name is not 'python' but the arguments are still a string. This will make the code more robust. [medium]


  - **relevant file:** base.py
   **suggestion content:** Add tests to ensure the bug is fixed and to prevent future regressions. This will improve the reliability of the code. [important]


  - **relevant file:** base.py
   **suggestion content:** Consider refactoring the code to avoid the use of hacks. This will improve the maintainability and readability of the code. [medium]


### How to use
> Tag me in a comment '@CodiumAI-Agent' to ask for a new review after you update the PR.

body:
I thought `python` was some default tool, but it turned out GPT is throwing out a random tool and I got this result from the agent
```
Function: python is not a valid tool, try another one.
```
Thus, the current fix is invalid but just let the code moving...

body:
So, is this just OpenAI's transient behaviors trying to use undefined
tools? Shouldn't we capture the errors by checking the tool names instead
of raising a unexpected parsing error? And possibly ask GPT to retry with a
different tool or natively answer?

I'd be happy to change this PR for that behavior.

body: my main concern is most of these integrations pages/notebooks were written by the owners of the integrations, and generally lean towards letting them own the content around their product
author: baskaryan

body:
> my main concern is most of these integrations pages/notebooks were written by the owners of the integrations, and generally lean towards letting them own the content around their product

Exactly. That's why I didn't change the notebooks in the code and explanation parts. I just added descriptions when they were missed and made the format consistent with our template.
 
 But we do not hold examples in the `ecosystem/integration` section. Pages in this section (let's call them the `Integration Cards` in terms of HuggingFace :) ) represent only top-level information: description, installation, classes based on this integration, and links to the class examples. 
 
To summarize: 
- `Examples` are created by the integration creators/owners with very little control on the content and a little more control on the format. `Examples` are saved into the correspondent Module folders. 
- `Integration cards` are under our control and have strict content and formatting rules. That allows us to automate our docs with these Cards.

author: baskaryan

body: @hopkins385 Why do you want to set the vector size externally? It should be derived from the embeddings, as far as I know.
author: kacperlukawski

body:
@baskaryan Ok, seems as if the pain to get rid of collection recreation was high enough that even two people provided PR's to achieve the same. Let me check in detail.

@kacperlukawski The current implementation makes an **additional** api call on **each** request to determine the vector size before saving the vectors. But in many cases the vector size (=dimensions) is known even before starting with the embedding process. In case of openai-embedding the vector-dimensions are 1536. So why performing an api call to determine the vector dimensions if the dimensions are already clear? It produces unnecessary costs and embedding delays.
author: hopkins385

body: Works as expected. Will create a new PR for the vector_size (dimensions).

body:
Hey @gauravrele87 ! How do you hope to use this? I believe you're calling an image generation model and getting a base64-encoded image back, which doesn't fit our LLM (text-to-text) abstraction particularly well.

Would a tool that generates images from the bedrock-stability endpoint be better?
author: efriis

body:
@efriis 
Thanks for reviewing this PR. This implementation returns a `base64` image as text output from the LLM class. We are expecting most users would want to write the image to filesystem for later access.

```python
from base64 import b64decode
img = b64decode(base64_data)
with open('image.png', 'wb') as f:
    f.write(img)
```

> Would a tool that generates images from the bedrock-stability endpoint be better?

Most tools I see so far are generating text output. Is there an example of a similar tool that generates non-text output? I am also envisioning using LLM class directly in the tool to call the Bedrock service, so having this code here will help. Let me know if you have a better way to handle this.
author: 3coins

body:
@baskaryan @efriis 
Let @gauravrele87 or me know if we can do anything to move this forward, or if there is a better way to implemented this. Thanks for your help.
author: 3coins

body:
Hey @3coins @gauravrele87 ! Let's skip merging this into the LLM class because it doesn't make sense in the LLM abstraction.

We haven't put much thought into image generation abstractions yet, so in the meantime, would you mind implementing it in a similar fashion to `langchain/tools/steamship_image_generation`?

It would be amazing if you could factor out shared logic between these Bedrock APIs into a shared `BedrockAPIWrapper` class that both the new `langchain.tools.bedrock_image_generation.BedrockStabilityTool` and existing/refactored `langchain.llms.bedrock.Bedrock` inherit from. Feel free to keep it in `langchain.llms.bedrock` or put it in `langchain.utilities.bedrock` - up to you!

author: rlancemartin

body:
Also, functionality here is:
1/ QA
2/ Translate

Looks like Doctran can [do more](https://github.com/psychic-api/doctran/tree/main) in terms of metadata extraction.

Plans to add that in a follow-up? We can discuss, too.

QA generation is cool, but impact is somewhat mild b/c we already have QAGeneration chain.

Adding context to chunks (in metadata) is the high impact addition that would be great to get in.
author: rlancemartin
jasonwcfan

body:
Added translate and QA as parsers, as well as metadata extraction since you mentioned that was valuable.  Will clean up the old files and update the notebooks early this week.

I ran into a couple of issues though:

**Passing arguments to parsers** 
The `BaseBlobParser` class doesn't support additional arguments when passed into loaders. It seems that other parsers don't need to accept additional arguments, but it's necessary for translation (need to specify the language) and metadata extraction (need to pass in a JSON schema to describe the metadata to extract). 

I can extend `BaseBlobParser` and `BaseLoader` to accept **kwargs if we want to keep these modules as parsers, but that might make the scope of these changes further reaching than anticipated.

```python
class DoctranTranslateParser(BaseBlobParser):
    """Translates text documents into other languages using doctran."""

    def lazy_parse(self, blob: Blob, language: str, **kwargs) -> Iterator[Document]:
        """Lazily parse the blob."""
        openai_api_key = get_from_dict_or_env(kwargs, "openai_api_key", "OPENAI_API_KEY")
        doctran = Doctran(openai_api_key=openai_api_key)
        doctran_doc = doctran.parse(content=blob.as_string()).translate(language=language).execute()
        yield [Document(page_content=doctran_doc.properties_as_yaml() if doctran_doc.extracted_properties else doctran_doc.transformed_content)]
```

**Splitting**
When splitting documents, the metadata for the parent document is copied to all child documents. This means any extracted properties (including QA pairs) will be copied over to all chunks, rather than mapped to only the relevant chunks that contain those properties. Therefore vector retrieval using these metadata properties won't be able to retrieve the correct documents if the document gets split.

I can:
1. Keep the functionality as is, but that would make metadata extraction useless if the user intends to split the document after parsing.
2. Keep the metadata extraction/QA generation step as a document transformation so it can happen after splitting
3. Add functionality to handle document splitting while mapping properties to the correct chunks. Not sure if there's a great way to do this since I'm not super familiar with the LangChain codebase yet.

Let me know what you think! Can also discuss more on our call but it's a lot to cover in 30 min haha.
author: jasonwcfan

body:
> @rlancemartin I might be missing something here, but is there an easy way to load strings into Blobs? Right now I have to define a new BlobLoader class in order to load strings:
> 
> ```python
> class StringBlobLoader(BlobLoader):
>     def yield_blobs(self) -> Iterable[Blob]:
>         yield Blob(data=sample_text)
> 
> loader = GenericLoader(
>     blob_loader=StringBlobLoader(),
>     blob_parser= DoctranTranslateParser(language="spanish")
> )
> docs = loader.load()
> ```

Blobs are files, so you can just put an example txt file w/ your string in [`../example data`](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/tree/master/docs/extras/modules/data_connection/document_loaders/integrations/example_data).
author: rlancemartin

body:
@rlancemartin Good to know! It wasn't a large amount of data so I just kept it in the notebook :)

All the changes are in!
* fixed the bug that was causing the issue in python <3.10, turns out it was due to the new Union notation using `|`.
* removed doctran as a dependency but will raise an `ImportError` if these new parsers are used without the package being installed

I think it should be good to merge in now, but let me know if I missed anything!

body:
- I have added memory to both SQLDatabaseChain and SQLDatabaseSequentialChain. So that user can do something like this:
```python

db = SQLDatabase.from_uri("connection_string")
llm = OpenAI(model_name="gpt-3.5-turbo-16k", temperature=0)
memory = ConversationBufferMemory(memory_key="history", input_key="input")
prompt = PromptTemplate(
    input_variables=["input", "table_info", "history"],
    template=_DEFAULT_TEMPLATE,
)
while True:
    db_chain = SQLDatabaseChain.from_llm(
        llm, db, memory=memory, prompt=prompt, verbose=True
    )
    db_chain.run(input())
```

- In order to make memory work with default prompts for SQL-Database Chain, I have added a function called `validate_sql_chain_memory` in newly created utils.py module for `sql_database` chains package. This makes sure that memory_key and input_key are **history** and **input** respectively otherwise it raises a `ValueError`.  
- Also added test for SQL-Database chains with and without memory.

body:
The use is something like this:

1- SQLDatabaseSequentialChain:

```python
_DEFAULT_TEMPLATE = """Given an input question, first create a syntactically correct {dialect} query to run, then look at the results of the query and return the answer. Unless the user specifies in his question a specific number of examples he wishes to obtain, always limit your query to at most {top_k} results. You can order the results by a relevant column to return the most interesting examples in the database.

Never query for all the columns from a specific table, only ask for a the few relevant columns given the question.

Pay attention to use only the column names that you can see in the schema description. Be careful to not query for columns that do not exist. Also, pay attention to which column is in which table.

Take into account past conversation history with the user before generating the SQL-Query:
{history}

Use the following format:

Question: Question here
SQLQuery: SQL Query to run
SQLResult: Result of the SQLQuery
Answer: Final answer here

"""

PROMPT = PromptTemplate(
    input_variables=["input", "table_info", "dialect", "top_k", "history"],
    template=_DEFAULT_TEMPLATE + PROMPT_SUFFIX,
)

_DECIDER_TEMPLATE = """Given the below input question, past conversation history and list of potential tables, output a comma separated list of the table names that may be necessary to answer this question.


History: {history}

Question: {input}

Table Names: {table_names}

Relevant Table Names:"""

DECIDER_PROMPT = PromptTemplate(
    input_variables=["input", "table_names", "history"],
    template=_DECIDER_TEMPLATE,
    output_parser=CommaSeparatedListOutputParser(),
)


# from typing import Any, Dict, List, Optional, Union

os.environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = ""
conn_string = ""
db = SQLDatabase.from_uri(conn_string)
llm = OpenAI(model_name="gpt-3.5-turbo-16k", temperature=0)
toolkit = SQLDatabaseToolkit(db=db, llm=llm)
memory = ConversationBufferMemory(memory_key="history", input_key="input")
agent =  SQLDatabaseSequentialChain.from_llm(llm = llm, database = db , query_prompt = PROMPT , decider_prompt = DECIDER_PROMPT ,memory = memory , verbose = True)
```
If the memory.memory_key is not in user-provided template then `validate_sql_chain_memory` will raise a error.

PS: Flow is similar for SQLDatabaseChain and SQL-Agent.

body: hmm im guessing for some models the text-generation endpoint does include the prompt? is there any good way to identify when that is the case?
author: baskaryan

body:
Since the response based on model-source is unpredictable, I think checking if the prompt is included is a good choice.

Example:
`gpt-2` retains the prompt in the returned response. Quick string compare is used to remove it:

```
generated text ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
[{'generated_text': 'Question: When does summer begin?\n\nAnswer: \xa0April 27. Some time in March. \nIn short, you should be able to have a little fun out on your patio by the end of March. I also recommend taking a trip'}]

remove prompt from response -------------------------------------------------------------

April 27. Some time in March.
In short, you should be able to have a little fun out on your patio by the end of March. I also recommend taking a trip
```
author: HashemAlsaket

body: the issue is that this import is being performed at top level, so if an exception is raised here then it will propagate anywhere this module is depended on. SelfQueryRetriever depends on this, and that's imported in langchain/retrievers/__init__, which i think means if someone didn't have Lark imported and they tried importing a totally unrelated Retriever via `from langchain.retrievers import ...` then they'd get this Lark import error.
author: baskaryan

@mpskex is attempting to deploy a commit to the **LangChain** Team on [Vercel](https://vercel.com).

A member of the Team first needs to [authorize it](https://vercel.com/git/authorize?team=LangChain&type=github&job=%7B%22headInfo%22%3A%7B%22sha%22%3A%2222f17d1610e1086c361dcdb74e1bfc9a3a3b48df%22%7D%2C%22id%22%3A%22Qmdgmo768Gvd5WU9H5ugDPhQsoNLyWUWsm2hhozMfkz3AM%22%2C%22org%22%3A%22langchain-ai%22%2C%22prId%22%3A7454%2C%22repo%22%3A%22langchain%22%7D).
author: vercel

body:
Got several cells conflicting in `langchain/docs/extras/modules/data_connection/retrievers/how_to/self_query/myscale_self_query.ipynb` 

## SQL Self-Query Retriever with MyScale

```python3
!pip3 install clickhouse-sqlalchemy InstructorEmbedding sentence_transformers openai langchain-experimental
```

```python3
from os import environ
import getpass
from typing import Dict, Any
from langchain import OpenAI, SQLDatabase, SQLDatabaseChain, LLMChain
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, Column, MetaData
from langchain import PromptTemplate


from sqlalchemy import create_engine

MYSCALE_HOST = "msc-1decbcc9.us-east-1.aws.staging.myscale.cloud"
MYSCALE_PORT = 443
MYSCALE_USER = "chatdata"
MYSCALE_PASSWORD = "myscale_rocks"
OPENAI_API_KEY = getpass.getpass("OpenAI API Key:")

engine = create_engine(
    f"clickhouse://{MYSCALE_USER}:{MYSCALE_PASSWORD}@{MYSCALE_HOST}:{MYSCALE_PORT}/default?protocol=https"
)
environ["OPENAI_API_KEY"] = OPENAI_API_KEY
```

```python3
from langchain.embeddings import HuggingFaceInstructEmbeddings
from langchain_experimental.sql.parser import VectorSQLOutputParser

output_parser = VectorSQLOutputParser.from_embeddings(
    model=HuggingFaceInstructEmbeddings(
        model_name="hkunlp/instructor-base", model_kwargs={"device": "cpu"}
    )
)
```

```python3
from langchain.callbacks import StdOutCallbackHandler

metadata = MetaData(bind=engine)

from langchain.llms import OpenAI
from langchain.chat_models import ChatOpenAI

from langchain_experimental.utilities.sql_database import SQLDatabase
from langchain_experimental.sql.prompt import MYSCALE_PROMPT
from langchain_experimental.sql.base import SQLDatabaseChain

chain = SQLDatabaseChain(
    llm_chain=LLMChain(
        llm=OpenAI(openai_api_key=OPENAI_API_KEY, temperature=0),
        prompt=MYSCALE_PROMPT,
        output_parser=output_parser,
    ),
    top_k=10,
    return_direct=True,
    database=SQLDatabase(engine, None, metadata),
    native_format=True,
)

import pandas as pd

pd.DataFrame(
    chain.run(
        "Please give me 10 papers to ask what is PageRank?",
        callbacks=[StdOutCallbackHandler()],
    )
)
```

## SQL Database as Retriever

```python3
from langchain.chains.qa_with_sources.retrieval import RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain

from langchain_experimental.retrievers.sql_database import SQLDatabaseChainRetriever
from langchain_experimental.sql.parser import VectorSQLRetrieveAllOutputParser

output_parser_retrieve_all = VectorSQLRetrieveAllOutputParser.from_embeddings(
    output_parser.model
)

chain = SQLDatabaseChain.from_llm(
    llm=OpenAI(openai_api_key=OPENAI_API_KEY, temperature=0),
    prompt=MYSCALE_PROMPT,
    top_k=10,
    return_direct=True,
    db=SQLDatabase(engine, None, metadata),
    sql_cmd_parser=output_parser_retrieve_all,
    native_format=True,
)

# You need all those keys to get docs
retriever = SQLDatabaseChainRetriever(sql_db_chain=chain, page_content_key="abstract")

document_with_metadata_prompt = PromptTemplate(
    input_variables=["page_content", "id", "title", "authors", "pubdate", "categories"],
    template="Content:\n\tTitle: {title}\n\tAbstract: {page_content}\n\tAuthors: {authors}\n\tDate of Publication: {pubdate}\n\tCategories: {categories}\nSOURCE: {id}",
)

chain = RetrievalQAWithSourcesChain.from_chain_type(
    ChatOpenAI(
        model_name="gpt-3.5-turbo-16k", openai_api_key=OPENAI_API_KEY, temperature=0.6
    ),
    retriever=retriever,
    chain_type="stuff",
    chain_type_kwargs={
        "document_prompt": document_with_metadata_prompt,
    },
    return_source_documents=True,
)
ans = chain("Please give me 10 papers to ask what is PageRank?")
print(ans["answer"])
```

body: Thanks for the comment! I have already migrated this into experimental. And I should have added a notebook to demonstrate what it is doing. I will be working on it now. Thanks for this again ❤️
author: mpskex

body:
> sorry for the delayed review
> 
> since this touches the sql/base file its going to be a bit harder to review than is probably worth it
> 
> the easiest way to move forward is probably to make this a completely standalone module in experimental. eg no changes to the existing files. i dont entirely understand the changes, but either way they should probably be done in a separate pr
> 
> an end-to-end example notebook would also be helpful here

I just added a notebook here: `docs/extras/modules/data_connection/retrievers/sql_database/myscale_vector_sql.ipynb`

body:
hey @rlancemartin — I realize this change is not needed. For more context, this change was made proactively in preparation for upcoming changes to our Starter (free) plan. It does not apply to our standard and enterprise plans

Could we revert this PR while we discuss a better way of handling this, thanks
author: jamescalam

body: +1 to @jamescalam's note. The change in [9d13dcd](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/commit/9d13dcd17c2dfab8f087bcc37e99f1181dfe5c63) is a breaking change and will cause users who currently initialize their Pinecone vector store with a `namespace` argument to face a `TypeError`. @rlancemartin, I recommend rolling back this change.

> it seems streaming mode does not work properly ?

Yes, As the low level api had not support  streaming mode when I implement this feature. I will do it as soon as possible.
author: wangxuqi

body:
> > it seems streaming mode does not work properly ?
> 
> Yes, As the low level api had not support streaming mode when I implement this feature. I will do it as soon as possible.

I added the following line in the `_generate()` function: 
"if run_manager: run_manager.on_llm_new_token(stream_resp["output"]["text"])". 
It allows the streaming callback to be called successfully, although there are still some minor issues present.

body: My team is no longer using this class so from my perspective I no longer need this merged, although others may benefit from it. In general, it is probably good to have a more robust parser and unit tests for it, but at this point (seven months after the PR was submitted) someone other than me should resolve the merge conflicts if they consider this worth having in langchain.

body: i think it probably makes sense to implement this as a DocumentTransformer rather than putting it on the base VectorStore. happy to help refactor if you'd like
author: baskaryan

body:
> i think it probably makes sense to implement this as a DocumentTransformer rather than putting it on the base VectorStore. happy to help refactor if you'd like
 
Yeap I started with that approach first then I realized that would imply re-write ton of code.
Basically every place you have a single retriever you will have to re-implement it as a Compressor, apply the new DocumentTransformer / filter etc, etc.
I was thinking that we need a more generic switch on /off for this since it's a pretty basic /low level tweak that affects all diff vector stores no matter what.
In the case of going with a document transformer how do we implement in a way that is less intrusive or avoid forcing a cascade of refactor everywhere?
author: GMartin-dev

@baskaryan Added it as a document transformer. I'm still not 100% sure this is the best approach.
I mean it works, but will force using compressors everywhere when you need a retriever.

body:
Also, in general: IIUC, this is a re-ordering of documents after retrieval (similar post-processing as something like Cohere re-rank). Re-rank is a vectorstore wrapper:

https://python.langchain.com/docs/modules/data_connection/retrievers/integrations/cohere-reranker

I'll have a look at the notebook tomorrow AM to get a better sense for usage.
author: rlancemartin

body:
> Also, in general: IIUC, this is a re-ordering of documents after retrieval (similar post-processing as something like Cohere re-rank). Re-rank is a vectorstore wrapper:
> 
> https://python.langchain.com/docs/modules/data_connection/retrievers/integrations/cohere-reranker
> 
> I'll have a look at the notebook tomorrow AM to get a better sense for usage.

Yeap this is my second implementation already it's hard to find the right place for this reordering. 
Currently as Document transformer, it's fuzzy for me the diff between transformer and compressors for this case... 
But since we do not need the query to run this reordering, it's totally **query agnostic**.
DocumentTransformer will do?

off topic (but related):
In case of a new simple embeddings reordering, for an scenario with merger retriever, check this comment:
https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/issues/3991#issuecomment-1609923453
In that case I think we need a DocumentCompressor since we need the query as input.

But in the case of the lost in the middle reordering we might need to apply it broadly before formatting the prompt, like for all context with more than 10 documents or something like that, since it's related to the **model** processing the prompt.
Right now it seems all state of the art models have this issue but some new architecture in the future could not needed.
author: GMartin-dev

body:
> > Also, in general: IIUC, this is a re-ordering of documents after retrieval (similar post-processing as something like Cohere re-rank). Re-rank is a vectorstore wrapper:
> > https://python.langchain.com/docs/modules/data_connection/retrievers/integrations/cohere-reranker
> > I'll have a look at the notebook tomorrow AM to get a better sense for usage.
> 
> Yeap this is my second implementation already it's hard to find the right place for this reordering. Currently as Document transformer, it's fuzzy for me the diff between transformer and compressors for this case... But since we do not need the query to run this reordering, it's totally **query agnostic**. DocumentTransformer will do?
> 
> off topic (but related): In case of a new simple embeddings reordering, for an scenario with merger retriever, check this comment: [#3991 (comment)](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/issues/3991#issuecomment-1609923453) In that case I think we need a DocumentCompressor since we need the query as input.
> 
> But in the case of the lost in the middle reordering we might need to apply it broadly before formatting the prompt, like for all context with more than 10 documents or something like that, since it's related to the **model** processing the prompt. Right now it seems all state of the art models have this issue but some new architecture in the future could not needed.

Ya, great point on `DocumentCompressor` vs `DocumentTransformer`. Compressors all have an explicit `compress_documents` method. In this case, you are right: no compression, so a `DocumentTransformer` is a better fit. But it's also a `DocumentTransformer` applied after retrieval. That's fine, the overall flow looks like this:

![image](https://github.com/hwchase17/langchain/assets/122662504/81147b0e-32d2-4728-b520-4a173798606d)

body:
## PR Analysis

- 🎯 **Main theme:** Adding a new document transformer that automatically extracts metadata for a document based on an input schema
- 🔍 **Description and title:** Yes
- 📌 **Type of PR:** Enhancement
- 🧪 **Relevant tests added:** No
- ✨ **Minimal and focused:** Yes, the PR is focused on adding a new feature of document metadata tagging and does not include unrelated changes.
- 🔒 **Security concerns:** No, the PR does not introduce possible security concerns or issues. The changes are related to data processing and do not involve any security-sensitive operations.
## PR Feedback

- 💡 **General PR suggestions:** The PR is well-structured and the code changes are clear. However, it lacks tests for the new functionality. It's important to add tests to ensure the new feature works as expected and to prevent regressions in the future. Additionally, the linter issue mentioned in the PR description should be resolved.

- 🤖 **Code suggestions:**


  - **relevant file:** langchain/document_transformers/__init__.py
   **suggestion content:** Consider adding type hints to the function `_filter_similar_embeddings` and `_filter_cluster_embeddings` for better code readability and maintainability. [important]


  - **relevant file:** langchain/document_transformers/openai_functions.py
   **suggestion content:** In the `MetadataTagger` class, the `atransform_documents` method raises a `NotImplementedError`. If this method is not intended to be used, consider removing it or adding a docstring to explain why it's not implemented. [medium]


  - **relevant file:** langchain/document_transformers/openai_functions.py
   **suggestion content:** In the `create_metadata_tagger` function, consider adding a docstring to explain the purpose of the function, its arguments, and its return value. This will improve code readability and maintainability. [medium]


  - **relevant file:** langchain/chains/openai_functions/tagging.py
   **suggestion content:** In the `create_tagging_chain` and `create_tagging_chain_pydantic` functions, consider adding a docstring to explain the purpose of the functions, their arguments, and their return values. This will improve code readability and maintainability. [medium]


### How to use
> Tag me in a comment '@CodiumAI-Agent' to ask for a new review after you update the PR.
> You can also tag me and ask any question, for example '@CodiumAI-Agent is the PR ready for merge?'

body:
> from my understanding, the `check_repeated_memory_variable` validator will raise an error if any of the variables in the `memories` list are repeated. However, the `load_memory_variables` method does not check for repeated variables. This means that it is possible for the `CombinedMemory` instance to return a dictionary of memory variables that contains duplicate values. This code will check for repeated variables in the `data` dictionary returned by the `load_memory_variables` method of each sub-memory. If a repeated variable is found, an error will be raised.

dictionaries can't contain duplicate keys, so current logic would actually overwrite the stuff in memory_data with data if there was a duplicate. which idk if that's desirable either. thoughts @hwchase17?

body:
hey @keenborder786, thanks for PR!

could you explain your use case a bit more? i don't think we want to pass memory to the LLMChain directly, since the AgentExecutor already takes care of keeping track of the agent's sequence of thoughts and passing those back to the LLM.
author: baskaryan

body:
Hey @baskaryan. 
When we create `AgentExecutor` from `create_sql_agent` or when you directly create the SQLChain from either `SQLDatabaseChain` or `SQLDatabaseSequentialChain`, I noticed there was no option of directly passing the memory whatsoever. Even though one of the [guides](https://python.langchain.com/docs/modules/agents/how_to/add_memory_openai_functions) shows how you can utilize memory through  OPENAI_FUNCTION agent but still giving the user the ability to pass in memory explicitly in both the SQL-agent and chain makes more sense.

Plus there were no tests for the sql-db-chains so I added them as well.

Cheers,
